1. Does this research actually leverage your AI agentic system-building capabilities?
Short answer:
Yes — but only if we explicitly reposition the project from “AI agents” to “Agent Operations (AgentOps)”.

Right now, your concept paper frames:

“design and implementation of a governed agentic automation platform”

That sounds right, but academically it is still tool-agnostic and capability-opaque. To leverage your strengths, the research must explicitly emphasise:

Agent orchestration

Workflow automation

Human-in-the-loop controls

Observability, logging, and governance

Lifecycle management (build → test → deploy → monitor)

These are not LLM skills. They are systems engineering + automation architecture skills — exactly where your Make / n8n / Airtable / API background sits.

🔑 Key reframing insight
You are not researching agents.
You are researching how agents are operationalised, governed, and scaled.

That is a legitimate, current, and under-researched problem.

2. What tools will you actually use to build this system?
Let’s be precise and honest — examiners and supervisors can smell vagueness.

Core tool stack (realistic, defensible)
Layer	Tooling	Why this is academically sound
Agent execution	n8n or Make	Proven workflow orchestration engines
Agent logic	LLM APIs (OpenAI / similar)	Treated as external services
Orchestration UI	Custom web app (React / simple frontend)	Demonstrates system boundary
Data layer	PostgreSQL / Airtable	Execution logs, configs, audit trails
Auth & roles	App-level RBAC	Enterprise governance requirement
Observability	Logs + run metadata	AgentOps core concept
This is not overengineering.
It is layer separation, which examiners love.

3. Should the AI agents be built in n8n or Make?
Analytically correct answer:
Yes — and no.

You should:

Build agents inside n8n / Make

Control them via a custom system

This mirrors real enterprise practice.

Why this is the right research decision
Workflow engines:

Already solve retries, triggers, error handling

Are battle-tested

Reduce implementation risk

Your research contribution is not reinventing orchestration, but:

Designing a governance, observability, and control layer around agent workflows.

That is novel in application, even if components exist.

4. Do n8n / Make allow cross-compatibility with custom systems?
Yes — explicitly and by design.

n8n
Webhooks (inbound & outbound)

REST API for workflow execution

External credential handling

Self-hosted (enterprise-friendly)

Make
Webhooks

API triggers

Scenario execution via HTTP

Tight SaaS integrations

This means:

Your platform does not run the agents
It orchestrates, governs, and observes them

This maps perfectly to systems theory:

n8n/Make = external subsystem

Your platform = core system

APIs = system boundary interfaces

8. Is there an actual problem in this niche? Where is the research?
Yes. This problem is well-documented, but fragmented across:

AI governance

MLOps

Autonomous agents

Human-AI collaboration

Below is foundational research you must cite.

Core Research Evidence (APA 7)
1. Autonomous agents & risks
Bender et al. (2021) warn that large language systems deployed without oversight introduce unpredictable behaviour and hidden failure modes.

Bender, E. M., et al. (2021). On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big? Proceedings of FAccT. https://doi.org/10.1145/3442188.3445922

2. Need for human-in-the-loop governance
Amershi et al. (2019) introduce human-AI interaction guidelines, stressing that AI systems must support oversight, error recovery, and transparency.

Amershi, S., et al. (2019). Guidelines for human-AI interaction. Proceedings of CHI. https://doi.org/10.1145/3290605.3300233

3. AI systems as socio-technical systems
Rahwan et al. (2019) argue that AI systems must be governed as complex socio-technical systems, not isolated tools.

Rahwan, I., et al. (2019). Machine behaviour. Nature, 568(7753), 477–486. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-019-1138-y

4. Operational AI governance
Kroll et al. (2017) highlight the lack of accountability mechanisms in algorithmic decision systems.

Kroll, J. A., et al. (2017). Accountable algorithms. University of Pennsylvania Law Review, 165(3), 633–705.

5. MLOps & production failures
Sculley et al. (2015) show how hidden technical debt accumulates when ML systems are deployed without operational controls.

Sculley, D., et al. (2015). Hidden technical debt in machine learning systems. NeurIPS.

📌 Research gap you occupy
Most work focuses on models or decisions.
Very little focuses on agent execution governance in workflow automation.

That’s your niche.

Step 3 — System Design & Diagrams Blueprint
(Context Diagram + Level 1 DFD + Use Case Diagram + what to draw + how tools fit)

This section is designed to make your diagrams easy to draw, easy to defend, and clearly compliant with APT3065’s compulsory design artefacts (Context Diagram, Level 1 DFD, Use Case Diagram). 

 


A. System Boundary (what is inside vs outside)
System (inside boundary): “AgentOps Control Tower”
Your custom platform that provides:

Agent Registry (configs, permissions, schemas)

Execution Control (trigger requests, approvals)

Governance (RBAC, policy gates)

Observability (run logs, metrics, history)

Audit & Reporting (who ran what, when, outcome)

External (outside boundary):
These are not built by you — they are interacted with via APIs/webhooks:

n8n OR Make (agent workflow runtime/orchestration)

LLM provider (e.g., OpenAI API; treated as an external service)

Marketing tools (Email service, Social platforms, Analytics, CRM)

Identity provider (optional) or just “Users” if you keep auth internal

This boundary is essential for systems theory + professional credibility: you own the control plane; orchestration engines and SaaS tools are the environment.

B. Architecture (what you are actually building)
Core components (MVP but enterprise-valid)
Web Dashboard (UI)

Admin console: register agents, set policies

Operator console: run agents, view logs

Viewer: read-only reporting

Backend API (Control Plane)

REST API for managing agents & runs

Approval workflow endpoints

Webhook receiver for n8n/Make run status

Database (Core data stores)

Agent registry store

Run history store

Audit log store

Outputs metadata store (links to stored artefacts)

Integration Layer

“Trigger workflow” calls to n8n/Make

Status callbacks from n8n/Make → your platform

Optional: connectors to email/analytics (but safer to run those inside n8n/Make)

This aligns with course expectations: planned system, documentation-ready, testable, demonstrable. 


C. Tooling decision: n8n vs Make (how it fits in diagrams)
Academic-safe choice
Use one engine for your implementation (pick either n8n or Make), and refer to the other as an alternative in “Future Work”.

Why both are compatible with custom systems (diagram-friendly)
Your platform triggers workflows via HTTP requests

Workflows call back with webhooks

That’s clean integration across system boundaries

So in your diagrams:

n8n/Make is an external entity/system

Data flows cross the boundary only via API calls + webhook callbacks

This is exactly how enterprise integrations are represented in DFD/Context models. 


D. Context Diagram Blueprint (what to draw)
Central process:
P0: AgentOps Control Tower

External Entities (boxes):
Admin User

Operator User

Viewer/Management User (optional but neat)

Workflow Runtime (n8n/Make)

LLM Provider (optional at context level; can be shown as part of workflow runtime to simplify)

Marketing Systems (Email/Analytics/CRM/Social as one combined external entity to avoid clutter)

Main data flows (arrows):
Admin → P0: Agent configuration / policy rules / user management

Operator → P0: Execution request / input payload

P0 → Operator: Run status / outputs / logs

Viewer → P0: Report request

P0 → Viewer: Dashboards / audit reports

P0 → n8n/Make: Trigger workflow + agent config + run ID

n8n/Make → P0: Run status callback + logs summary + output metadata

n8n/Make ↔ Marketing Systems: Send email / fetch analytics / post content / update CRM

n8n/Make ↔ LLM Provider: Prompt + tool context / response

Defence line: This shows the system boundary clearly and the organisation’s environment influence (external tools) vs internal control (governance).

E. Level 1 DFD Blueprint (what to draw)
You’ll decompose P0 into 5–6 processes, with 4 data stores.

Processes (circles):
P1: Manage Users & Roles (RBAC)

creates accounts/roles (Admin/Operator/Viewer)

P2: Register & Configure Agents

agent name, purpose, allowed tools, schema, policy flags

versioning (v1, v2) if possible

P3: Request Agent Execution

validates input, checks permissions

P4: Approval & Policy Gate (Human-in-the-loop)

approvals required for risky actions (e.g., sending emails, posting publicly)

P5: Trigger Workflow Runtime (n8n/Make)

sends execution request with run ID + configuration

P6: Monitor & Record Execution (Observability)

ingests callback logs, statuses, output metadata

generates run dashboards and reports

Data Stores (open-ended rectangles):
D1: Users & Roles Store
D2: Agent Registry Store
D3: Run History & Logs Store
D4: Audit Trail Store (separate from logs — this distinction reads “enterprise”)

External entities connected:
Admin User, Operator User, Viewer User

Workflow Runtime (n8n/Make)

DFD flows (what connects to what):
Admin → P1 → D1

Admin → P2 → D2

Operator → P3 (execution request)

P3 reads D1 (permissions) and D2 (agent config)

If approval required → P4 → (approve/deny)

P4 writes D4 (audit record)

P5 sends execution request to n8n/Make

n8n/Make returns status + output metadata to P6

P6 writes D3 (logs/run history) and D4 (audit)

Viewer reads P6 outputs (reports/dashboard)

Defence line: This DFD demonstrates structured process modelling and controlled data flows, matching the course’s emphasis on modelling and documentation.

Step 4 — Methodology + Evaluation + QA + Evidence Plan
(with the interoperability design baked in)

4.1 Research / Development Approach
This is an applied systems development project, best framed as:

Approach: Design Science + SDLC (Iterative)
Design Science: you build an artefact (AgentOps platform) to solve a real organisational problem, then evaluate it.

Iterative SDLC: you build in increments, validate, refine.

This also fits your course content emphasis on research, analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation. 

 


4.2 Core Innovation: Runtime-Independent Agent Execution (Interoperability)
The enterprise problem (vivid)
Right now, organisations adopt automation platforms in fragments:

Team A uses Make

Team B uses n8n

Team C uses scripts and manual steps

AI agents become non-portable and non-governable across environments.

Your “Aha” solution
You propose:

A governed AgentOps platform that can execute agent workflows through multiple runtimes using a standard adapter interface, producing consistent audit logs and observability regardless of runtime.

This is the project’s “pop”.

4.3 System Design Strategy for Interoperability
The key pattern: Adapter Layer
You define a standard internal interface:

RuntimeAdapter

trigger_run(agent_id, input_payload) -> run_id

get_status(run_id) -> status

receive_callback(run_id, status, logs, outputs)

fetch_run_logs(run_id) -> logs

Then implement two adapters:

MakeAdapter (webhook trigger + callback handler)

N8nAdapter (webhook trigger + callback handler)

What you gain academically
Clear modular architecture (enterprise characteristic: maintainability + scalability) 


Strong subsystems + boundary clarity (systems theory)

Evaluation and comparison becomes easy (you can measure differences)

4.4 Data Collection & System Inputs (Methodology detail)
Your system will use:

Inputs
Agent configuration (stored in Agent Registry)

Execution requests from Operators (date range, campaign IDs, KPI choices)

Runtime execution logs (callback data)

Outputs
Agent output artefacts (report text, file links, KPI summary)

Execution status (success/failure, duration)

Audit logs (who ran what, when, approvals)

These map perfectly to systems theory input-output-feedback loops.

MVP Definition (non-negotiable)

Multi-tenant workspaces

Marketplace

Many agents

Many tool integrations

Those go under “Future Work”, consistent with project scope expectations.

Successful run rate

Audit completeness

B) Interoperability proof (Make vs n8n)
Metrics:

Time to integrate each runtime (build adapter)

Reliability: callback consistency

Latency differences (time to completion)

Error patterns (where failures occur)

You are not claiming “one is better universally”; you’re proving:

the platform design is runtime-independent

governance remains consistent across runtimes

That’s enterprise-grade thinking.

4.8 Risk Management (scope control)
Major risks

LLM variability in outputs

run_id, status, started_at, finished_at, logs[], outputs[]

Start with one simple agent workflow

Keep one data source (static dataset or simple API)

Treat LLM as external and log prompts + response metadata

CONCEPT PAPER
Course: APT3065 – Business & Visual Analytics
Semester: Spring 2026

Project Title
Design and Implementation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform for Governed AI Automation: An Interoperability Study Using Make and n8n in Marketing Operations

1. Introduction and Background
The rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) agents has transformed how organisations automate knowledge-intensive tasks such as reporting, analytics, content generation, and decision support. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agents to reason over unstructured data and orchestrate multi-step workflows with minimal human intervention. However, as organisations operationalise these agents, critical challenges have emerged around governance, transparency, reliability, and scalability.

In practice, AI agents are often deployed through fragmented automation environments—such as isolated scripts, low-code workflow tools, or ad-hoc integrations—without a unified control plane. This fragmentation results in limited visibility into who triggered an agent, what tools it accessed, what data it processed, how it behaved during execution, and whether it failed silently. Prior research warns that AI systems deployed without adequate oversight and accountability mechanisms introduce operational risk and hidden failure modes (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015).

Furthermore, enterprise environments frequently rely on multiple automation runtimes concurrently (e.g., Make and n8n), leading to agent lock-in and inconsistent governance practices across teams. While workflow engines are effective for execution, they lack native, organisation-wide capabilities for auditability, policy enforcement, and cross-runtime observability. This gap motivates the need for an Agent Operations (AgentOps) platform that governs agent execution independently of the underlying runtime.

This project proposes the design and implementation of a runtime-independent AgentOps control platform that governs AI agent execution across heterogeneous automation environments. The platform is evaluated through a marketing operations case study, demonstrating interoperability between Make and n8n while maintaining consistent governance, observability, and audit trails.

2. Problem Statement
Organisations deploying AI agents across heterogeneous automation platforms lack a standardised, runtime-independent system for governing agent execution, monitoring behaviour, and maintaining auditability. As a result, AI agents operate with limited visibility, inconsistent controls, and weak accountability across workflows, increasing operational risk and constraining enterprise-scale adoption.

3. Objectives of the Study
3.1 General Objective
To design and implement a runtime-independent AgentOps platform that enables governed execution, observability, and auditability of AI agents across multiple automation runtimes in enterprise operations.

3.2 Specific Objectives
i. To examine governance and accountability challenges associated with operational AI agents in enterprise automation environments.
ii. To design a modular AgentOps architecture that decouples agent governance from workflow execution runtimes.
iii. To implement an adapter-based prototype enabling interoperable agent execution across Make and n8n.
iv. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed platform using a governed marketing operations workflow.

4. Research Questions
i. What governance and observability challenges arise when AI agents are deployed across heterogeneous automation runtimes?
ii. How can systems analysis and design techniques be applied to create a runtime-independent AgentOps platform?
iii. To what extent does the proposed platform improve execution transparency, accountability, and reliability in a marketing operations context?

5. Methodology
This study adopts a design science research approach combined with an iterative systems development life cycle (SDLC). The research involves requirements analysis, architectural design, prototype implementation, and evaluation. Systems analysis and design artefacts—including context diagrams, data flow diagrams, and use case diagrams—will be used to model the platform.

The prototype will implement a runtime adapter layer that standardises agent execution requests and callbacks across Make and n8n. A single governed marketing operations agent (weekly performance reporting) will be executed through both runtimes, with execution metadata, logs, and outputs captured centrally. Evaluation will involve functional testing, structured walkthroughs, and user acceptance testing, focusing on observability, audit completeness, and interoperability consistency (Amershi et al., 2019).

6. Expected Outcomes and Significance
The project is expected to deliver a functional AgentOps prototype demonstrating runtime-independent governance of AI agents. Key outcomes include improved execution transparency, consistent audit trails across runtimes, and reduced operational risk. Academically, the study contributes to emerging work on operational AI governance by extending principles from MLOps and human-AI interaction to agentic workflow automation (Rahwan et al., 2019).

Practically, the proposed platform provides a foundation for enterprise-grade, subscription-based AgentOps systems capable of supporting multiple operational domains beyond marketing, without vendor lock-in. The findings highlight the importance of decoupling agent governance from execution infrastructure to enable scalable and responsible AI adoption.

References (APA 7)
Amershi, S., Weld, D., Vorvoreanu, M., Fourney, A., Nushi, B., Collisson, P., … Horvitz, E. (2019). Guidelines for human–AI interaction. Proceedings of the 2019 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1–13. https://doi.org/10.1145/3290605.3300233

Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A., & Shmitchell, S. (2021). On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big? Proceedings of the ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 610–623. https://doi.org/10.1145/3442188.3445922

Rahwan, I., Cebrian, M., Obradovich, N., Bongard, J., Bonnefon, J.-F., Breazeal, C., … Wellman, M. (2019). Machine behaviour. Nature, 568(7753), 477–486. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-019-1138-y

Sculley, D., Holt, G., Golovin, D., Davydov, E., Phillips, T., Ebner, D., … Dennison, D. (2015). Hidden technical debt in machine learning systems. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 28.

Course: APT3065 – Business & Visual Analytics

Project Title
Design and Evaluation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform for Governed AI Automation in Marketing Workflows

Concept Paper
Organisations are increasingly adopting Artificial Intelligence (AI) to automate operational tasks such as reporting, analytics, and content management. In marketing operations, AI-powered workflows are used to analyse campaign performance, generate reports, and support decision-making. However, in many organisations, these AI-driven workflows are executed using different automation platforms without a unified mechanism for control, monitoring, or accountability.

As a result, it is often difficult to determine who initiated an AI workflow, how the workflow was executed, what tools were accessed, and whether the workflow completed successfully. The absence of governance and visibility introduces operational risks, reduces trust in AI-generated outputs, and limits the ability of organisations to scale AI automation effectively.

This project proposes the design and evaluation of a governed Agent Operations (AgentOps) platform that enables controlled execution, monitoring, and auditing of AI-driven workflows in marketing operations. The platform is designed to operate independently of the underlying automation runtime, allowing the same governed workflow to be executed across different automation platforms.

The study focuses on a single marketing workflow—weekly performance reporting—to demonstrate how the proposed platform can provide consistent execution control, audit logging, and observability when workflows are executed using two different automation tools. By evaluating the system across multiple runtimes, the project demonstrates how governance and accountability can be maintained regardless of the execution environment.

The expected outcome of the project is a functional prototype that improves transparency, control, and reliability of AI-driven marketing workflows. The findings of the study will provide practical insights into how organisations can govern AI automation more effectively and lay the foundation for future development of scalable, subscription-based AgentOps platforms.

End of Concept Paper

Project Title
Design and Evaluation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform for Governed AI Automation in Marketing Workflows

1. Introduction and Background
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly used by organisations to automate operational tasks such as reporting, analytics, and content management. In marketing operations, AI-driven workflows are commonly applied to analyse campaign performance, generate reports, and support planning decisions. These workflows are often implemented using automation platforms that integrate multiple tools and data sources.

However, many organisations operate multiple automation platforms concurrently, and AI-driven workflows are executed without a central mechanism for control, monitoring, or accountability. As a result, it becomes difficult to determine who initiated a workflow, how it was executed, what tools were accessed, and whether the workflow completed successfully. This lack of visibility reduces trust in AI-generated outputs and introduces operational risks.

Although automation platforms such as Make and n8n are effective at executing workflows, they do not provide organisation-wide governance features such as audit trails, execution approval, and cross-platform monitoring. This creates challenges when AI workflows need to be managed consistently across different environments.

This project proposes the design and evaluation of an Agent Operations (AgentOps) platform that governs AI-driven marketing workflows independently of the underlying automation runtime. The platform is evaluated using a marketing operations case study, where the same governed workflow is executed across Make and n8n while maintaining consistent control, monitoring, and audit logging.

2. Problem Statement
Organisations executing AI-driven marketing workflows across different automation platforms lack a unified system for governing workflow execution, monitoring behaviour, and maintaining audit records. This results in limited visibility, weak accountability, and increased operational risk, making it difficult to manage and scale AI automation effectively.

3. Objectives of the Study
3.1 General Objective

To implement a prototype that executes a governed marketing workflow across Make and n8n.

CONCEPT PAPER
Course: APT3065 VA – Mid-Term Project
Semester: Spring 2026
Instructor Name: Prof. Chrispus Alukwe
Student Name: Dustine Kibagendi
Student ID: 670797

Project Title
Design and Evaluation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform for Governed AI Automation in Marketing Workflows

1. Introduction and Background
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly used by organisations to automate operational tasks such as reporting, analytics, and content management. In marketing operations, AI-driven workflows are applied to analyse campaign performance, generate reports, and support planning decisions. However, as AI systems are deployed into real organisational workflows, they introduce operational challenges related to reliability, accountability, and oversight—especially when outputs are used to inform decisions and actions (Rahwan et al., 2019).

In many organisations, AI-driven workflows are executed using different automation platforms without a central mechanism for control, monitoring, or accountability. This fragmentation makes it difficult to determine who initiated a workflow, what tools it accessed, what data it processed, and whether it failed silently—an issue that increases operational risk and reduces trust in AI-generated outputs (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015). Research on production AI systems also highlights that operational complexity and “hidden technical debt” can accumulate when systems are built and scaled without strong operational controls and monitoring structures (Sculley et al., 2015).

Although automation platforms such as Make and n8n can execute workflows effectively, they do not consistently provide organisation-wide governance features such as audit trails, execution approvals, and cross-platform observability. Human–AI interaction guidelines further emphasise that AI-enabled systems should support user oversight, recovery from errors, and transparency in system behaviour (Amershi et al., 2019). These issues motivate the need for an Agent Operations (AgentOps) platform that enables governed execution and monitoring of AI-driven workflows independent of the underlying automation runtime.

This project proposes the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform that governs AI-driven marketing workflows. The platform will be evaluated using a marketing operations case study, where the same governed workflow is executed across Make and n8n while maintaining consistent control, monitoring, and audit logging.

2. Problem Statement
Organisations executing AI-driven marketing workflows across different automation platforms lack a unified system for governing workflow execution, monitoring behaviour, and maintaining audit records. This results in limited visibility, weak accountability, and increased operational risk, making it difficult to manage and scale AI automation effectively (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015).

3. Objectives of the Study
3.1 General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform that enables controlled execution, monitoring, and auditing of AI-driven marketing workflows.

3.2 Specific Objectives
To identify governance and visibility challenges associated with AI-driven workflows in marketing operations (Amershi et al., 2019; Rahwan et al., 2019).

To design an AgentOps platform that separates workflow governance from automation runtime execution (Sculley et al., 2015).

To implement a prototype that executes a governed marketing workflow across Make and n8n and records standardised execution logs and audit entries (Sculley et al., 2015).

To evaluate the platform based on execution transparency, auditability, and workflow reliability across both runtimes (Amershi et al., 2019; Bender et al., 2021).

4. Research Questions
What governance and monitoring challenges arise when AI-driven marketing workflows are executed across different automation platforms (Amershi et al., 2019; Rahwan et al., 2019)?

How can a runtime-independent AgentOps platform be designed to govern AI-driven marketing workflows while enabling consistent audit logging and monitoring (Sculley et al., 2015)?

How effective is the proposed platform in improving execution transparency and accountability across Make and n8n (Bender et al., 2021; Amershi et al., 2019)?

5. Methodology
This study adopts a design science research approach combined with an iterative systems development life cycle (SDLC). The project will involve analysing workflow governance requirements, designing the AgentOps platform, implementing a functional prototype, and evaluating its performance.

Systems analysis and design tools—including context diagrams, data flow diagrams, and use case diagrams—will be used to model the system. A single marketing workflow (weekly performance reporting) will be executed through both Make and n8n. Execution logs, audit records, and workflow outcomes will be analysed to evaluate whether the platform improves observability and accountability across runtimes, consistent with recommendations on oversight and operational robustness in AI-enabled systems (Amershi et al., 2019; Sculley et al., 2015).

6. Expected Outcomes and Significance
The project is expected to produce a functional prototype demonstrating how AI-driven marketing workflows can be governed consistently across different automation platforms. The platform will provide improved visibility into workflow execution, clear audit records, and better operational control over AI-enabled processes (Amershi et al., 2019). By reducing fragmentation and improving monitoring, the system can mitigate risks associated with unobserved failures and uncontrolled behaviour, which are commonly raised concerns in deployed AI systems (Bender et al., 2021; Rahwan et al., 2019).

The findings will be significant in demonstrating a practical approach to governing AI automation in organisational settings and may inform future development of scalable AgentOps platforms for other operational domains.

References (APA 7)
Amershi, S., Weld, D., Vorvoreanu, M., Fourney, A., Nushi, B., Collisson, P., … Horvitz, E. (2019). Guidelines for human–AI interaction. Proceedings of the 2019 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1–13. https://doi.org/10.1145/3290605.3300233

Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A., & Shmitchell, S. (2021). On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big? Proceedings of the ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 610–623. https://doi.org/10.1145/3442188.3445922

Sculley, D., Holt, G., Golovin, D., Davydov, E., Phillips, T., Ebner, D., … Dennison, D. (2015). Hidden technical debt in machine learning systems. In C. Cortes, N. D. Lawrence, D. D. Lee, M. Sugiyama, & R. Garnett (Eds.), Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (Vol. 28).

CONCEPT PAPER
Course: APT3065 VA – Mid-Term Project
Semester: Spring 2026
Instructor Name: Prof. Chrispus Alukwe
Student Name: Dustine Kibagendi
Student ID: 670797

Project Title
Design and Evaluation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform for Governed AI Automation in Marketing Workflows

1. Introduction and Background
Artificial Intelligence is increasingly being used by organisations to support and automate operational tasks such as reporting, analytics, and content management. In marketing operations, AI-driven workflows are commonly applied to analyse campaign performance, generate summaries, and assist in planning and decision-making. These workflows are often built using automation platforms that connect multiple tools and data sources.

However, as AI-driven workflows become more common in organisational settings, challenges have emerged around control, transparency, and accountability. In many organisations, AI workflows are executed across different automation platforms without a central mechanism for monitoring or governance. This makes it difficult to establish who initiated a workflow, what data and tools were accessed, how decisions were made during execution, and whether the workflow completed as intended. Research has shown that AI systems deployed without adequate oversight can introduce operational risks and hidden failure modes that are difficult to detect and manage (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015).

Marketing teams are particularly affected by this problem because they frequently rely on time-sensitive AI workflows that draw data from multiple platforms and generate outputs that inform strategic decisions. While automation tools such as Make and n8n are effective at executing workflows, they do not consistently provide organisation-wide features for audit logging, execution approval, or cross-platform monitoring. Human–AI interaction research further highlights the importance of maintaining user oversight, system transparency, and the ability to recover from errors when AI systems are embedded in real-world workflows (Amershi et al., 2019).

This project proposes the design and evaluation of an Agent Operations (AgentOps) platform that enables governed execution of AI-driven marketing workflows. The platform is designed to operate independently of the underlying automation runtime, allowing the same governed workflow to be executed across different automation platforms while maintaining consistent monitoring and audit records.

2. Problem Statement
Organisations executing AI-driven marketing workflows across different automation platforms lack a unified system for governing workflow execution, monitoring system behaviour, and maintaining audit records. As a result, these workflows operate with limited visibility and weak accountability, increasing operational risk and making it difficult to manage and scale AI automation effectively (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015).

3. Objectives of the Study
3.1 General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform that enables controlled execution, monitoring, and auditing of AI-driven marketing workflows.

3.2 Specific Objectives
To identify governance and visibility challenges associated with AI-driven workflows in marketing operations (Amershi et al., 2019; Rahwan et al., 2019).

To design an AgentOps platform that separates workflow governance from automation runtime execution (Sculley et al., 2015).

To implement a prototype that executes a governed marketing workflow across Make and n8n while recording standardised execution logs and audit records.

To evaluate the platform based on execution transparency, auditability, and workflow reliability across both automation platforms (Amershi et al., 2019; Bender et al., 2021).

4. Research Questions
What governance and monitoring challenges arise when AI-driven marketing workflows are executed across different automation platforms (Amershi et al., 2019; Rahwan et al., 2019)?

How can a runtime-independent AgentOps platform be designed to govern AI-driven marketing workflows while maintaining consistent monitoring and audit logging (Sculley et al., 2015)?

How effective is the proposed platform in improving execution transparency and accountability when the same workflow is executed using Make and n8n (Bender et al., 2021)?

5. Methodology
This study adopts a design science research approach combined with an iterative systems development life cycle. The project involves analysing workflow governance requirements, designing the AgentOps platform, implementing a functional prototype, and evaluating its performance.

Systems analysis and design tools, including context diagrams, data flow diagrams, and use case diagrams, will be used to model the proposed system. A single marketing workflow, weekly performance reporting, will be implemented and executed using both Make and n8n. Execution logs, audit records, and workflow outcomes will be analysed to assess whether the platform improves observability, accountability, and reliability across automation runtimes, in line with recommendations for oversight and robustness in deployed AI systems (Amershi et al., 2019; Sculley et al., 2015).

6. Expected Outcomes and Significance
The project is expected to produce a functional prototype that demonstrates how AI-driven marketing workflows can be governed consistently across different automation platforms. The platform will provide improved visibility into workflow execution, clear audit records, and stronger operational control over AI-enabled processes (Amershi et al., 2019).

Academically, the study contributes to ongoing discussions on operational AI governance by applying established systems analysis principles to agent-driven automation. Practically, the findings will offer a structured approach for organisations seeking to manage AI workflows responsibly and may inform future development of scalable AgentOps platforms for use beyond marketing operations.

CHAPTER ONE
INTRODUCTION
1.1 Background of the Study
Artificial Intelligence has become an integral part of organisational operations, particularly in areas that require continuous data processing, reporting, and decision support. In marketing operations, AI-driven workflows are widely used to analyse campaign performance, generate insights, automate reporting, and support strategic planning. These workflows often rely on automation platforms that integrate multiple tools, data sources, and services to deliver results efficiently.

As organisations adopt AI automation at scale, operational challenges related to governance, transparency, and accountability have become more pronounced. AI-driven workflows frequently operate across different automation platforms, each with its own execution environment and monitoring capabilities. This fragmentation makes it difficult to maintain consistent oversight over how workflows are initiated, executed, and completed. Research on deployed AI systems shows that such complexity can introduce hidden failure modes, reduce trust in system outputs, and increase operational risk when oversight mechanisms are weak or absent (Sculley et al., 2015; Bender et al., 2021).

Marketing teams are particularly exposed to these challenges because their workflows are often time-sensitive and decision-critical. AI-generated reports and analyses directly influence budget allocation, campaign adjustments, and strategic decisions. Despite this, many automation platforms prioritise execution efficiency over governance features such as audit logging, approval controls, and cross-platform visibility. Studies on human–AI interaction emphasise that AI systems used in real-world settings must support transparency, user oversight, and error recovery in order to be reliable and trustworthy (Amershi et al., 2019).

Recent research has also highlighted the need to treat AI systems not only as technical artefacts but as operational actors whose behaviour must be observed, managed, and evaluated within organisational contexts (Rahwan et al., 2019). This perspective has given rise to the concept of Agent Operations, commonly referred to as AgentOps, which focuses on the governance, monitoring, and lifecycle management of AI agents in production environments.

Within this context, there is a growing need for systems that can govern AI-driven workflows independently of the platforms on which they are executed. A runtime-independent approach allows organisations to maintain consistent control, monitoring, and accountability even when workflows are deployed across multiple automation environments. This study addresses this need by proposing and evaluating a runtime-independent AgentOps platform applied to marketing workflows.

1.2 Statement of the Problem
Organisations that execute AI-driven marketing workflows across different automation platforms lack a unified mechanism for governing workflow execution, monitoring system behaviour, and maintaining audit records. As a result, these workflows operate with limited visibility and weak accountability, making it difficult to trace execution history, detect failures, and assess reliability. This lack of governance increases operational risk and limits organisations’ ability to scale AI automation responsibly (Sculley et al., 2015; Bender et al., 2021).

1.3 Objectives of the Study
1.3.1 General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform that enables controlled execution, monitoring, and auditing of AI-driven marketing workflows.

1.3.2 Specific Objectives
To examine governance and visibility challenges associated with AI-driven workflows in marketing operations.

To design an AgentOps platform that separates workflow governance from automation runtime execution.

To implement a prototype that executes a governed marketing workflow across Make and n8n while recording standardised execution logs and audit records.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and workflow reliability across both automation platforms.

1.4 Research Questions
What governance and monitoring challenges arise when AI-driven marketing workflows are executed across different automation platforms?

How can a runtime-independent AgentOps platform be designed to govern AI-driven marketing workflows while maintaining consistent monitoring and audit logging?

How effective is the proposed platform in improving execution transparency and accountability when the same workflow is executed using Make and n8n?

1.5 Significance of the Study
This study is significant both academically and practically. From an academic perspective, it contributes to existing research on AI governance and operational oversight by applying systems analysis and design principles to agent-driven automation in a real organisational context. The study also responds to calls in the literature for greater attention to the operational behaviour of AI systems deployed in production environments (Rahwan et al., 2019).

From a practical perspective, the findings of this study will provide organisations with a structured approach for governing AI-driven workflows across multiple automation platforms. By demonstrating how execution control, monitoring, and auditability can be maintained independently of the underlying runtime, the project offers insights that can support more reliable and accountable use of AI in marketing operations and beyond (Amershi et al., 2019; Bender et al., 2021).

1.6 Scope of the Study
The study focuses on the design and evaluation of a single AI-driven marketing workflow, specifically weekly performance reporting. The workflow will be implemented and executed using two automation platforms, Make and n8n, to demonstrate runtime independence. The project does not aim to develop a full enterprise-scale system but rather a functional prototype that illustrates key governance and monitoring capabilities.

1.7 Limitations of the Study
The study is limited to a single workflow and two automation platforms, which may restrict the generalisability of the findings to other domains or platforms. In addition, the evaluation focuses on governance and operational visibility rather than quantitative performance metrics such as execution speed or cost. These limitations are acknowledged and provide opportunities for future research.

1.8 Organisation of the Study
The remainder of the study is organised as follows. Chapter Two presents a review of related literature on AI governance, AgentOps, and operational challenges in AI systems. Chapter Three describes the methodology and system design. Chapter Four presents the implementation and evaluation of the proposed platform. Chapter Five concludes the study and outlines recommendations for future work.

References
Amershi, S., Weld, D., Vorvoreanu, M., Fourney, A., Nushi, B., Collisson, P., and Horvitz, E. (2019). Guidelines for human–AI interaction. Proceedings of the 2019 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 1–13. https://doi.org/10.1145/3290605.3300233

Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A., and Shmitchell, S. (2021). On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big? Proceedings of the ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 610–623. https://doi.org/10.1145/3442188.3445922

CHAPTER ONE
INTRODUCTION

This project examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in marketing workflows. The study focused on addressing governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven workflows that were executed across multiple automation platforms. The proposed system was designed to operate independently of specific automation runtimes while maintaining consistent monitoring, audit logging, and execution control.

1.1 Background of the Study

Organisations increasingly relied on Artificial Intelligence to automate operational tasks such as performance reporting, campaign analytics, and content management. Within marketing operations, workflows were typically implemented using automation platforms that integrated data sources, APIs, and external services to produce analytical reports and decision-support outputs.

In the current operational environment examined in this study, marketing workflows were executed using automation tools such as Make and n8n. These tools enabled efficient workflow orchestration but handled execution monitoring independently within their own environments. Workflow logs, execution history, and system behavior were platform-specific and not centrally governed.

As AI-driven workflows became more complex and decision-critical, this operational structure introduced visibility gaps. It became difficult to trace who initiated a workflow, what data sources were accessed, whether execution errors occurred, and how outputs were generated. Research has shown that inadequate oversight in deployed AI systems can introduce hidden technical debt and operational risks that accumulate over time (Sculley et al., 2015; Bender et al., 2021). Human–AI interaction studies further emphasised the need for transparency and oversight when AI systems influence real-world decisions (Amershi et al., 2019).

This study was therefore grounded in the need to improve governance and accountability in AI-driven marketing workflows by introducing a runtime-independent AgentOps control layer.

1.2 Problem Statement

Marketing workflows in the examined operational environment were executed across different automation platforms without a unified governance mechanism. Each platform maintained its own execution logs and monitoring structures, which resulted in fragmented visibility.

This fragmentation initiated several operational problems:

First, workflow execution history was not centrally traceable, making accountability difficult.

Second, failures within one automation environment were not easily observable outside that environment.

Third, audit records were inconsistent across platforms, limiting reliability assessments.

These operational conditions increased organisational risk and constrained the responsible scaling of AI automation. There was therefore a need to design a unified governance mechanism capable of monitoring and auditing AI-driven workflows across heterogeneous automation runtimes.

1.3 Objectives

1.3.1 General Objective

To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in marketing workflows.

1.3.2 Specific Objectives

i. To investigate governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

ii. To design a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture that separated governance control from automation runtime execution.

iii. To develop and deploy a functional prototype that executed a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across Make and n8n within the project implementation period.

iv. To analyze the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and reliability across both automation environments.

1.4 Research Questions

i. What governance and monitoring challenges were associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms?

ii. How could a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture be designed to govern workflow execution independently of specific automation runtimes?

iii. How was a governed marketing workflow implemented across Make and n8n using the proposed platform?

iv. To what extent did the proposed platform improve execution transparency and audit consistency across both environments?

1.5 Significance of the Study

This study was significant because it addressed a timely and practical challenge in the operational deployment of AI systems. As organisations increasingly adopted AI-driven workflows, governance and accountability became critical concerns. The project presented an opportunity to integrate knowledge from systems analysis, automation engineering, and AI governance principles.

The study contributed academically by applying structured systems design techniques to the emerging domain of AgentOps. Practically, it provided a prototype model that organisations could adapt to improve oversight and control of AI-driven workflows.

1.6 Scope and Limitation of the Study

The study covered the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent governance platform applied to a single marketing workflow, specifically weekly performance reporting. The workflow was implemented and tested using two automation platforms, Make and n8n.

The study did not cover large-scale enterprise deployment, performance benchmarking, or integration with additional automation platforms beyond the two selected environments. These limitations were due to time constraints and the scope defined for the undergraduate project.

CHAPTER ONE
INTRODUCTION
This project examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in marketing workflows. The study addressed governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven workflows executed across multiple automation platforms within contemporary organisations. The proposed system introduced a control layer that operated independently of specific automation runtimes while maintaining consistent execution monitoring and audit logging.

1.1 Background of the Study
Contemporary organisations increasingly relied on Artificial Intelligence to automate operational processes such as campaign analytics, performance reporting, and customer engagement tracking. Within marketing operations, automation platforms were commonly used to integrate data from advertising systems, customer databases, analytics dashboards, and reporting tools.

These automation platforms enabled efficient orchestration of multi-step workflows. However, each platform typically maintained its own execution environment and logging mechanisms. Workflow monitoring, error reporting, and audit history were therefore confined to the specific platform on which the workflow was executed.

As marketing workflows became more dependent on AI-driven processing and decision support, the absence of unified governance created operational challenges. It became difficult to trace workflow initiation, monitor execution behaviour across platforms, and maintain consistent audit records. Research has indicated that insufficient oversight in deployed AI systems can result in hidden technical debt, reliability issues, and increased operational risk (Sculley et al., 2015; Bender et al., 2021). Additionally, effective human–AI interaction requires transparency and system observability to maintain trust and accountability (Amershi et al., 2019).

Within a generic marketing operations environment, where multiple automation platforms are used concurrently, there was a need for a unified governance mechanism capable of overseeing AI-driven workflows independently of the underlying runtime infrastructure.

1.2 Problem Statement
In contemporary marketing operations environments, AI-driven workflows were executed across multiple automation platforms without a central governance mechanism. Each platform stored execution logs independently and provided monitoring capabilities limited to its own runtime.

This operational structure resulted in fragmented visibility, inconsistent audit records, and limited cross-platform monitoring. When workflow failures occurred, tracing execution history required manual inspection within each platform. This reduced transparency and increased the risk of undetected errors influencing marketing decisions.

The absence of a unified governance layer therefore limited organisational accountability and constrained the responsible scaling of AI automation. A runtime-independent mechanism was required to monitor, audit, and control AI-driven workflows consistently across heterogeneous automation platforms.

1.3 Objectives
1.3.1 General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment.

1.3.2 Specific Objectives
i. To investigate governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

ii. To design a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture that separated governance control from workflow execution runtimes.

iii. To develop and deploy a functional prototype that executed a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across Make and n8n within the defined project implementation period.

iv. To analyze the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and reliability across both automation environments.

1.4 Research Questions
i. What governance and monitoring challenges were associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms?

ii. How could a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture be designed to govern AI-driven workflows independently of specific automation runtimes?

iii. How was a governed marketing reporting workflow implemented across Make and n8n using the proposed platform?

iv. To what extent did the proposed platform improve execution transparency and audit consistency across both automation platforms?

1.5 Significance of the Study
This study addressed a practical and timely challenge in the deployment of AI systems within organisational environments. As marketing operations increasingly relied on automated and AI-driven workflows, governance and accountability became critical for ensuring reliability and informed decision-making.

The project contributed academically by applying structured systems analysis and design principles to the operational governance of AI workflows. Practically, it demonstrated how organisations could implement a runtime-independent oversight mechanism capable of supporting responsible AI automation across heterogeneous platforms.

1.6 Scope and Limitation of the Study
The study focused on the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform applied to a single marketing workflow, specifically weekly performance reporting. The workflow was implemented and tested using two automation platforms, Make and n8n.

The study did not cover enterprise-wide deployment, integration with additional automation platforms, or long-term operational scaling. Performance benchmarking and cost optimisation were also outside the scope of the project. These limitations were defined in accordance with the time and structural constraints of the undergraduate project.

CHAPTER ONE
INTRODUCTION
This project examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The study focused on the operational challenges that emerged when Artificial Intelligence driven workflows were executed across multiple automation platforms without a unified governance mechanism. As organisations increasingly integrated AI into marketing analytics, reporting, and decision-support processes, the need for structured oversight, transparency, and cross-platform observability became more critical.

Marketing operations historically relied on manual data consolidation, spreadsheet-based reporting, and human interpretation of campaign metrics. Over time, the growth of digital advertising ecosystems, customer data platforms, and real-time analytics tools introduced greater complexity into marketing workflows. To manage this complexity, organisations adopted automation platforms capable of orchestrating multi-step processes involving data extraction, transformation, AI analysis, and reporting.

Although these platforms improved efficiency and reduced manual workload, they were primarily designed for execution rather than governance. Each automation environment maintained its own internal logs, triggers, and error-handling mechanisms. When AI-driven processes were layered into these workflows, oversight became fragmented. Monitoring was confined to individual platforms, audit trails were inconsistent, and accountability was difficult to centralise.

The absence of unified governance created operational blind spots. When failures occurred or outputs were questioned, teams were required to investigate across multiple systems independently. This approach increased operational risk and reduced organisational confidence in AI-generated outputs. Research on production AI systems has shown that weak monitoring structures and insufficient observability contribute to hidden technical debt and long-term reliability concerns (Sculley et al., 2015). Furthermore, scholarship on AI ethics and accountability has highlighted the risks of deploying automated decision-support systems without adequate oversight mechanisms (Bender et al., 2021; Rahwan et al., 2019).

Within this context, the concept of Agent Operations, commonly referred to as AgentOps, emerged as an operational discipline focused on governing AI agents in production environments. AgentOps extends principles from DevOps and MLOps by emphasising lifecycle management, monitoring, logging, and accountability for AI-driven systems. However, many existing implementations remained tied to specific platforms or execution environments.

This project therefore addressed the need for a runtime-independent governance layer capable of supervising AI-driven marketing workflows across heterogeneous automation platforms. By designing and evaluating such a platform, the study aimed to demonstrate how consistent audit logging, execution transparency, and cross-platform observability could be achieved without dependence on a single automation runtime.

1.1 Background of the Study
Contemporary organisations operated within highly dynamic marketing environments characterised by large volumes of structured and unstructured data. Marketing teams relied on performance metrics such as conversion rates, engagement statistics, return on advertising spend, and audience segmentation analytics. These metrics were derived from multiple digital channels including search advertising, social media platforms, email campaigns, and customer relationship management systems.

To manage this complexity, automation platforms were increasingly adopted to integrate and coordinate marketing processes. These platforms enabled organisations to design workflows that extracted data from external systems, transformed it into structured formats, applied AI-based analysis, and generated reports for managerial review. In many cases, Large Language Models and other AI systems were integrated into workflows to summarise performance insights, detect anomalies, and generate recommendations.

Within a generic contemporary marketing operations environment, it was common for organisations to use more than one automation platform concurrently. This situation occurred for several reasons, including cost considerations, legacy system compatibility, feature differences, and departmental preferences. For example, one team might use one platform for campaign reporting automation while another team used a different platform for customer segmentation workflows.

Although such environments enabled flexibility, they also introduced fragmentation. Each platform maintained separate execution histories, logging formats, and monitoring dashboards. AI-driven workflows executed within one platform were not inherently visible within another. Consequently, organisational oversight was distributed rather than centralised.

Research has emphasised that AI systems deployed in operational contexts require robust monitoring frameworks to ensure reliability and accountability (Amershi et al., 2019). When AI-generated outputs directly influence business decisions, insufficient transparency can reduce trust and increase the likelihood of undetected errors. Furthermore, studies on machine behaviour have argued that AI systems should be treated as actors within organisational ecosystems whose behaviour must be observed and governed systematically (Rahwan et al., 2019).

In marketing operations, AI-generated summaries and analytics frequently informed strategic decisions such as budget reallocation, targeting adjustments, and campaign optimisation. If AI-driven workflows malfunctioned, produced incomplete outputs, or accessed incorrect data sources, the consequences could extend beyond technical inconvenience to strategic misdirection.

Despite these risks, governance mechanisms often remained embedded within individual automation platforms rather than implemented at an organisational level. This meant that monitoring capabilities were limited to the specific environment in which the workflow was executed. Cross-platform observability required manual consolidation of logs and data, which was inefficient and prone to oversight gaps.

The background of this study was therefore grounded in the recognition that contemporary marketing operations environments required a unified governance approach for AI-driven workflows. Such an approach needed to operate independently of specific automation runtimes while maintaining consistent auditability and execution transparency.

1.2 Problem Statement
In contemporary marketing operations environments, AI-driven workflows were executed across multiple automation platforms without a unified governance framework. Each platform stored execution logs independently and provided monitoring tools limited to its own runtime environment.

This operational configuration produced several interrelated problems.

First, workflow execution history was fragmented. When stakeholders sought to trace how a report was generated or which data sources were accessed, they were required to inspect logs within each platform separately. There was no centralised audit trail capable of providing a consolidated execution narrative.

Second, monitoring was inconsistent across platforms. One platform might provide detailed logs and error notifications, while another might offer minimal observability. This inconsistency reduced the reliability of oversight and created uneven accountability structures.

Third, failure detection was reactive rather than proactive. If an AI-driven workflow produced incorrect or incomplete outputs, identification of the failure depended largely on human review rather than systematic cross-platform monitoring.

Fourth, governance policies such as approval workflows, access controls, and audit retention rules were implemented differently across platforms. This variability limited the organisation’s ability to enforce consistent standards.

These problems originated directly from the way marketing operations were currently handled. Workflows were implemented independently within each automation platform based on functional convenience rather than governance architecture. As a result, governance was an afterthought rather than a foundational design component.

The absence of a runtime-independent governance layer increased operational risk, reduced transparency, and constrained the responsible scaling of AI automation. There was therefore a need to design and evaluate a platform capable of providing unified monitoring, audit logging, and execution control across heterogeneous automation environments.

1.3 Objectives
1.3.1 General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment.

1.3.2 Specific Objectives
i. To investigate governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

ii. To design a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture that separated governance control from workflow execution runtimes.

iii. To develop and deploy a functional prototype that executed a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across two automation platforms within the project implementation period.

iv. To analyze the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and reliability across both automation environments.

Each objective was specific, measurable through system artefacts and evaluation criteria, achievable within the defined project timeframe, relevant to contemporary AI governance challenges, and bounded by the undergraduate project duration.

1.4 Research Questions
i. What governance and monitoring challenges were associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms?

ii. How could a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture be designed to govern AI-driven workflows independently of specific automation runtimes?

iii. How was a governed marketing reporting workflow implemented across two automation platforms using the proposed platform?

iv. To what extent did the proposed platform improve execution transparency, audit consistency, and workflow reliability across both automation environments?

These research questions were derived directly from the specific objectives and guided both system design and evaluation phases.

1.5 Significance of the Study
The significance of this study was both theoretical and practical.

From a theoretical perspective, the study extended existing research on AI governance by operationalising governance principles within a systems design framework. While prior scholarship had identified risks associated with unmonitored AI systems (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015), fewer studies had translated these concerns into concrete architectural designs within automation ecosystems. By conceptualising and implementing a runtime-independent AgentOps platform, the study bridged conceptual governance discourse and practical systems engineering.

From a practical perspective, the study addressed a timely organisational challenge. As AI-driven workflows became embedded in marketing operations, organisations required structured mechanisms to ensure accountability and transparency. The proposed platform demonstrated how governance could be decoupled from execution infrastructure, thereby enabling consistent oversight even in multi-platform environments.

The project also provided an opportunity to integrate knowledge acquired throughout the undergraduate programme, including systems analysis, database design, software engineering principles, and automation technologies. It required independent investigation, structured design reasoning, and implementation discipline, thereby fulfilling the academic objectives outlined in the project guideline document 

.

1.6 Scope and Limitation of the Study
The study focused on the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform applied to a single AI-driven marketing workflow, specifically weekly performance reporting. The workflow involved data aggregation, AI-generated summarisation, and structured output generation.

The implementation environment included two automation platforms selected to represent heterogeneous runtimes. The system architecture, logging mechanisms, and governance controls were developed and tested within this defined context.

The study did not include enterprise-scale deployment across multiple departments, nor did it integrate additional automation platforms beyond the two selected environments. Long-term performance benchmarking, cost analysis, and large-scale user adoption studies were outside the scope of the project. Additionally, the study did not evaluate AI model accuracy or retraining processes, as its focus remained on governance and operational oversight rather than model optimisation.

These limitations were defined to ensure feasibility within the academic timeframe and structural requirements of the undergraduate project.

ABSTRACT
ABSTRACT

This study examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. As organisations increasingly adopted Artificial Intelligence to automate marketing analytics and reporting workflows, governance and monitoring challenges emerged due to fragmented execution across multiple automation platforms.

The study identified limitations in cross-platform visibility, audit consistency, and execution transparency within AI-driven marketing workflows. To address these challenges, a runtime-independent governance architecture was designed to operate as a control layer separate from specific automation runtimes. A functional prototype was developed and deployed to execute a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across two automation platforms.

The system was evaluated based on its ability to improve execution transparency, maintain consistent audit logs, and enhance workflow reliability across heterogeneous environments. The findings demonstrated that decoupling governance from execution infrastructure improved monitoring consistency and strengthened operational accountability.

This study contributes to the emerging field of Agent Operations by applying structured systems analysis and design principles to AI workflow governance in organisational contexts.

Keywords: AgentOps, AI governance, marketing automation, runtime independence, workflow monitoring, audit logging

ADDITION TO CHAPTER ONE
1.7 System Architecture Overview
The proposed system was designed as a modular, layered architecture intended for implementation within a generic contemporary marketing operations environment. The architecture was structured to ensure scalability, transparency, and cross-platform operability.

The system consisted of five architectural layers.

1.7.1 Input Layer
This layer captured structured and unstructured marketing inputs including campaign briefs, content requests, performance parameters, and external platform triggers. Standardised intake forms and API triggers were used to ensure consistent data entry. This layer reduced ambiguity in workflow initiation and ensured traceability from the point of origin.

1.7.2 Orchestration Layer
The orchestration layer coordinated workflow execution across automation platforms. It handled task routing, conditional logic, approval sequencing, and synchronization with data stores. This layer was implemented using workflow automation platforms capable of API-based triggering and logging.

1.7.3 Processing and Intelligence Layer
This layer incorporated AI-enabled components responsible for content summarisation, tagging, anomaly detection, and performance interpretation. It enhanced workflow outputs by integrating machine-driven analysis while remaining governed by the orchestration layer.

1.7.4 Data and Knowledge Layer
This layer maintained structured databases containing campaign data, workflow logs, performance metrics, and archival knowledge assets. It supported audit traceability, reporting, and institutional learning.

1.7.5 Output and Feedback Layer
This layer generated reports, dashboards, alerts, and executive summaries. It ensured that workflow outcomes were communicated clearly and that performance insights informed subsequent marketing actions.

The architecture was intentionally modular to allow governance to operate independently of specific automation runtimes. This design supported runtime independence while maintaining consistent monitoring and audit logging across heterogeneous platforms.

Now we proceed to Chapter Two.

CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW
This chapter critically examined literature relevant to the governance of AI-driven marketing workflows and the architectural foundations of automation systems. The review was structured around the specific objectives of the study and concluded with a conceptual framework that informed system design.

2.1 Review of Objective One
Governance and Monitoring Challenges in AI-Driven Marketing Workflows

Research has increasingly highlighted the risks associated with deploying AI systems without structured oversight. Bender et al. (2021) warned that large language systems, when embedded in operational workflows, can produce outputs that appear authoritative despite underlying uncertainty. This creates accountability challenges when outputs influence decision-making.

Similarly, Sculley et al. (2015) introduced the concept of hidden technical debt in machine learning systems. They argued that complexity accumulates when monitoring, logging, and maintenance structures are insufficient. In marketing automation contexts, such technical debt may manifest as inconsistent performance metrics, undocumented workflow modifications, and fragmented audit histories.

Amershi et al. (2019) emphasised that AI systems integrated into human workflows must support transparency and error recovery. Without clear logging and user oversight, trust in AI-generated outputs diminishes. In marketing environments, where AI-generated summaries inform budget allocation and campaign adjustments, such trust is critical.

These studies collectively demonstrate that AI-driven workflows require structured governance frameworks to ensure reliability, traceability, and accountability.

2.2 Review of Objective Two
Architectural Separation of Governance and Execution

The separation of governance control from execution infrastructure is grounded in systems engineering principles. Modular system design enhances maintainability, scalability, and fault isolation. Rahwan et al. (2019) argued that AI systems should be treated as socio-technical actors whose behavior must be observed within broader operational ecosystems.

In automation engineering, layered architectures are widely adopted to isolate data processing, orchestration, and presentation layers. This separation improves system clarity and reduces dependency coupling. Workflow automation literature also supports decoupling orchestration logic from data storage and intelligence modules to enhance flexibility.

Applying these principles to AI governance suggests that oversight mechanisms should not be embedded exclusively within individual execution platforms. Instead, governance should operate as a cross-cutting layer capable of supervising heterogeneous runtimes.

2.3 Review of Objective Three
Interoperability and Multi-Platform Automation

Contemporary marketing environments frequently employ multiple automation tools due to cost structures, departmental autonomy, and feature differentiation. Interoperability has therefore become a central concern in business process orchestration research.

Studies on enterprise integration highlight the importance of API-driven communication, middleware layers, and standardised logging formats to enable cross-platform coordination. Without standardisation, workflow fragmentation leads to inconsistent data structures and monitoring gaps.

Low-code automation platforms have gained popularity due to accessibility and rapid deployment capabilities. However, literature suggests that such platforms often prioritise ease of use over governance architecture. Consequently, cross-platform consistency requires additional design considerations beyond default platform capabilities.

The proposed architecture addressed this gap by introducing a runtime-independent governance layer that standardised logging and audit functions across heterogeneous automation environments.

2.4 Review of Objective Four
Evaluation of Transparency and Reliability

Evaluation of AI-enabled systems must consider both technical performance and governance effectiveness. Transparency refers to the ability to trace execution pathways, access logs, and interpret system behavior. Reliability refers to consistent workflow performance and accurate reporting.

Human–AI interaction research underscores that systems perceived as transparent are more likely to be trusted and adopted (Amershi et al., 2019). Furthermore, operational monitoring frameworks improve fault detection and system robustness.

In marketing operations, transparent workflows enhance decision confidence. Reliable audit trails support compliance and performance review processes. Therefore, evaluating improvements in transparency and reliability provided measurable indicators of governance effectiveness.

Automation platform environment

The conceptual relationship suggests that implementing a runtime-independent governance layer improves transparency, strengthens audit consistency, and enhances workflow reliability within marketing operations.
