CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW
2.0 Introduction
This chapter critically reviewed literature relevant to the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in marketing workflows. The review was structured around the specific objectives of the study and focused on scholarly and standards-based work that justified the need for governance, monitoring, auditability, and cross-platform interoperability in AI-driven automation environments.

The study was grounded in the reality that contemporary marketing operations increasingly relied on automation tools and AI systems to execute performance reporting, campaign analytics, and decision-support tasks. As these systems became embedded in operational workflows, literature consistently highlighted risks relating to reliability, accountability, transparency, and long-term maintenance. Scholars also indicated that operational AI systems accumulated hidden complexity over time, particularly when monitoring and governance were not treated as core design requirements (Sculley et al., 2015). 

In parallel, standards bodies and regulators increasingly expected organisations to implement governance structures that supported traceability, documentation, and ongoing monitoring for AI systems, especially those that influenced business decisions (NIST, 2023; ISO/IEC 42001:2023; EU AI Act). 

This literature review therefore examined the operational governance problem in marketing automation, evaluated architectural strategies that separated governance from execution, assessed the interoperability challenge across multiple automation platforms, and reviewed approaches used to evaluate transparency and reliability in AI-enabled workflow systems.

2.1 Review of Objective One
Governance and Monitoring Challenges in AI-Driven Marketing Workflows
Objective One investigated governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

A consistent theme in literature was that AI systems deployed into operational environments introduced risk not only through model limitations but through the way AI outputs were operationalised. In marketing operations, AI-generated insights often informed campaign decisions, budget allocation, segmentation strategies, and messaging adjustments. This created a situation where incorrect, biased, or inconsistent outputs could translate into real operational and financial consequences.

A foundational concern was the nature of AI outputs and how they could appear authoritative even when they were uncertain or inaccurate. Literature on large language models warned that fluent generation could mask limitations in factual reliability, accountability, and transparency (Bender et al., 2021). 
 In marketing environments, where AI systems summarised performance results or suggested strategic directions, this risk increased the need for governance mechanisms that enabled teams to audit outputs and trace their provenance.

The concept of hidden technical debt in machine learning systems further strengthened the case for governance. Sculley et al. (2015) argued that machine learning systems accumulated maintenance burdens through entanglement, feedback loops, configuration complexity, and weak monitoring practices. 
 In practice, this meant that as marketing workflows grew, the effort required to debug failures, verify outputs, and maintain stable performance increased. When monitoring was fragmented across platforms, this debt was amplified because teams had to rely on manual investigation and inconsistent logs.

Human–AI interaction research also supported the need for transparency and oversight in AI-enabled workflows. Amershi et al. (2019) proposed guidelines for how AI systems should behave in human decision contexts, including communicating system status, supporting recovery from errors, and maintaining clarity about what the system was doing. 
 Marketing automation workflows often operated in the background, meaning that users did not always see intermediate steps or failure points. This reduced transparency, and it made error recovery difficult, particularly when workflows were distributed across multiple platforms.

At an organisational level, governance frameworks emphasised structured oversight functions. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework described AI risk management through core functions that included governance, mapping system context, measuring risk, and managing risk. 
 From an operational perspective, this placed emphasis on ongoing monitoring, documentation, and accountability mechanisms rather than one-time model evaluation. ISO/IEC 42001 similarly outlined the expectation that organisations established an AI management system with policies, risk management processes, lifecycle controls, and continuous improvement. 

Regulatory expectations also reinforced the need for logs, documentation, and traceability. The EU AI Act included requirements tied to documentation retention and automatically generated logs for certain classes of AI systems. 
 Even when marketing automation systems were not categorised as “high-risk” in a regulatory sense, the direction of policy made it clear that traceability, documentation, and oversight were becoming baseline expectations for responsible AI operations.

Marketing automation literature further contextualised the governance problem by showing how marketing automation adoption expanded rapidly but often created process fragmentation and accountability gaps. A recent systematic analysis of marketing automation literature described definitional fragmentation and categorisation complexity in the domain, which implied that organisations implemented automation in varied ways and often without standardised governance approaches. 
 Related work connected marketing automation to accountability and measurement of marketing return, reinforcing the importance of trustworthy, traceable reporting outputs. 

These studies collectively supported the argument that AI-driven marketing workflows required governance mechanisms that strengthened traceability, monitoring, and accountability. The literature gap was not the absence of automation tools, but the absence of a unified operational control layer that could govern workflows consistently across heterogeneous execution environments.

2.2 Review of Objective Two
Designing Runtime-Independent Governance Through Architectural Separation
Objective Two focused on designing a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture that separated governance control from workflow execution runtimes.

The architectural principle underpinning this objective was separation of concerns. Workflow automation platforms generally provided orchestration capabilities, but governance features such as cross-platform auditing, consistent observability, and standardised policy enforcement were often limited or tied to the platform’s internal environment. As a result, governance became fragmented whenever organisations used multiple automation runtimes.

Literature on machine behaviour supported the idea that AI systems should be observed as actors operating within complex ecosystems. Rahwan et al. (2019) argued that understanding AI behaviour required a broad research agenda that included monitoring and control mechanisms. 
 Translating this idea into system design implied that AI agents and AI-enabled workflows needed a governance layer that recorded behaviour, enforced boundaries, and enabled accountability.

In operational AI contexts, architecture was not only about building functionality but about building control. The combination of AI systems with automation platforms created a socio-technical system where human oversight, workflow logic, and AI outputs interacted. The literature therefore supported a design approach where governance was implemented as a layer that could remain stable even when workflow runtimes changed.

This argument aligned with broader governance standards and frameworks. The NIST AI RMF explicitly emphasised governance as a foundation for risk management, rather than a secondary task. 
 ISO/IEC 42001 similarly required organisations to implement lifecycle management and oversight processes, which would be difficult to maintain if governance was fragmented across multiple tools. 

In addition, research and practitioner literature on AI agents increasingly highlighted risks associated with autonomy and tool access. A Reuters analysis of AI agents described risks including erratic behaviour, privacy violations, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and misalignment from developer intent when agents interacted with systems without continuous oversight. 
 While this work was presented in a legal context, it reinforced a general point: as AI systems gained agency through integration with tools, governance and monitoring became essential operational controls.

The architectural implication was clear. A governance layer needed to exist above or alongside execution engines, not inside them. By making governance runtime-independent, organisations could standardise audit logs, approvals, and monitoring even while workflows executed in different platforms. This also supported scalability because organisations could adopt new automation runtimes or migrate workflows without rewriting governance logic.

This study therefore aligned its architecture with the literature by proposing an AgentOps control layer that governed execution, recorded audit trails, and maintained consistency across different runtimes. It treated the automation platform as an execution engine, while the AgentOps platform acted as a policy and observability layer.

2.3 Review of Objective Three
Interoperability and Multi-Platform Automation in Marketing Operations
Objective Three addressed the development and deployment of a functional prototype that executed a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across Make and n8n within the project implementation period.

The literature strongly suggested that interoperability was a practical requirement in modern operations. Organisations rarely used a single tool for all workflow automation due to differences in cost, integration capabilities, legacy constraints, departmental autonomy, and varying technical preferences. In marketing operations, this was especially common because different teams often owned different systems including CRM tools, analytics suites, ad platforms, and email automation tools.

Business Process Management research supported the view that automation systems improved efficiency but faced adoption challenges when process governance was unclear. A case-based study of Business Process Management System implementation highlighted how BPMS adoption influenced process orientation and organisational effectiveness, implying that process design and governance played key roles in sustaining operational performance. 

Inter-organisational process governance research also strengthened the need for governance and interoperability mechanisms. A 2024 study on modelling inter-organisational business process governance incorporated governance activities such as IT performance measurement and data governance operations, reinforcing that governance was not only a technical issue but also an organisational coordination issue. 
 Although this study focused on broader inter-organisational contexts, its findings aligned with the multi-platform marketing context, where governance needed to span more than one system.

Workflow management research historically emphasised audit trail data as essential for process monitoring and analysis. Classic workflow monitoring studies described audit trails as fundamental data sources for understanding process behaviour and diagnosing issues. 
 This was directly relevant to this project because the proposed system relied on standardised audit logs and consistent monitoring across platforms.

Marketing automation research further justified why the workflow selected for implementation, weekly marketing performance reporting, was appropriate and useful. Marketing performance reporting represented a recurring operational workflow that consumed multi-source data and produced outputs that influenced decisions. AI in marketing literature described AI’s role in marketing as spanning data analysis, decision support, and automation of marketing operations, indicating that AI-enabled reporting was a realistic and high-value use case. 

The interoperability challenge became more urgent when AI was embedded into workflows. AI components introduced new failure modes such as hallucinated summaries, inconsistent classifications, and unpredictable costs due to token usage and repeated retries. This meant that interoperability in AI workflows was not only about moving data across systems. It was also about ensuring that AI behaviours and outputs were traceable and governed across the entire workflow chain.

Recent enterprise reporting suggested that many agentic AI initiatives struggled to move from pilots into production due to governance, security, and observability gaps. While these reports were not academic, they reflected widespread organisational constraints that aligned with scholarly risk arguments. 
 This strengthened the rationale for implementing a prototype that demonstrated operational governability as a central value, rather than focusing only on AI capability.

Therefore, the prototype developed in this study was designed to show that a governed workflow could execute across Make and n8n while maintaining consistent logging, approvals, and monitoring outputs. This directly addressed the interoperability gap highlighted in literature and reflected a realistic operational environment where tool heterogeneity was normal.

2.4 Review of Objective Four
Evaluating Transparency, Auditability, and Reliability in AI-Enabled Workflows
Objective Four analysed the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and reliability across both automation environments.

The literature emphasised that evaluating AI-enabled systems required more than accuracy measurements. In operational contexts, trust and adoption depended on transparency, observability, and governance. Human–AI interaction guidelines supported evaluation approaches that emphasised system feedback, explainability, and error recovery. 
 This meant that the study’s evaluation needed to focus on whether users could trace workflow execution, understand outcomes, and recover from failures.

LLM observability research increasingly formalised design principles for monitoring AI behaviour in production systems. A 2025 ACM article on LLM observability presented developer-centric principles across the lifecycle, supporting the idea that logs, tracing, and evaluation were necessary for trustworthy deployment of language model systems. 
 This aligned strongly with the AgentOps framing because an AgentOps platform essentially operationalised observability and governance for AI workflows.

Governance standards reinforced the need for documentation and records. EU AI Act materials highlighted requirements for documentation keeping and automatically generated logs, reflecting an expectation that AI system operation should be traceable and auditable. 
 Even outside regulated “high-risk” settings, these requirements supported the study’s selection of audit logging and monitoring as evaluation criteria, because they reflected the direction of accountability standards.

NIST AI RMF also supported a continuous evaluation perspective, where risk management was iterative and required measurement and monitoring over time. 
 This reinforced the concept that governance effectiveness could be evaluated through the consistency of logs, clarity of monitoring outputs, and ability to detect and respond to failures.

In practical operational terms, transparency was measured by whether the system provided a coherent execution narrative. This included knowing what triggered the workflow, what steps ran, what data was accessed, what AI prompts were used, and what outputs were produced. Auditability was measured by whether logs were consistently captured and stored in a standardised format across both runtimes. Reliability was measured by whether workflows executed consistently without silent failures, and whether failures were detected and recorded in ways that enabled corrective action.

In summary, the evaluation criteria selected for this study aligned strongly with both scholarly work and governance standards. The literature did not treat observability and monitoring as optional features. It treated them as necessary conditions for safe, scalable, and trustworthy AI-enabled automation.

CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW
2.0 Introduction
This chapter critically reviewed literature relevant to the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The review was structured according to the specific objectives of the study and examined four key domains: AI governance in operational contexts, architectural separation of control and execution, interoperability across heterogeneous automation platforms, and evaluation of transparency and reliability in AI-enabled workflow systems.

Rather than describing literature in isolation, this chapter analysed areas of agreement, identified limitations in current approaches, and articulated the research gap addressed by this study. The review demonstrated that although governance frameworks, automation systems, and AI observability principles had been extensively discussed, there remained limited translation of these concepts into executable architectural designs for cross-platform marketing automation environments.

2.1 Governance and Monitoring Challenges in AI-Driven Marketing Workflows
The first objective examined governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

Literature on AI system deployment consistently highlighted that risks did not arise solely from model design but from operational embedding. Bender et al. (2021) argued that large language systems could produce outputs that appeared coherent and authoritative while masking uncertainty or error. This phenomenon created accountability risks when such outputs were integrated into decision-making environments.

In marketing operations, AI-generated summaries, anomaly detection outputs, and performance interpretations directly influenced managerial actions. When AI systems were embedded within automated workflows, tracing output origin and execution logic became increasingly complex. Sculley et al. (2015) introduced the concept of hidden technical debt in machine learning systems, explaining how monitoring gaps, entangled dependencies, and configuration complexity accumulated long-term maintenance burdens.

However, while these studies identified risks, they primarily focused on model lifecycle and engineering debt rather than cross-platform workflow governance. They did not address the operational reality in which marketing teams used multiple low-code automation tools simultaneously.

Human–AI interaction research further emphasised transparency and observability as essential for trust and effective use (Amershi et al., 2019). Yet most guidelines focused on user-facing transparency rather than backend execution traceability across heterogeneous systems.

Governance frameworks such as the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and ISO/IEC 42001 emphasised organisational oversight, documentation, and lifecycle management. These frameworks provided high-level governance principles but did not prescribe concrete architectural patterns for ensuring consistent monitoring across multiple automation runtimes.

The literature therefore revealed a critical limitation: governance was discussed at policy and risk levels, while operational implementation details within multi-platform marketing automation environments remained underexplored.

This study responded directly to that gap.

2.2 Architectural Separation of Governance and Execution
The second objective involved designing a runtime-independent architecture that separated governance control from execution runtimes.

Systems engineering principles have long supported modular design and separation of concerns. Layered architectures reduce coupling, improve maintainability, and isolate faults. In workflow automation systems, orchestration engines typically handle task routing and execution logic, while data storage and presentation layers are managed separately.

However, governance has often been embedded within execution environments. In low-code automation platforms, monitoring dashboards and logs remain confined to the platform in which the workflow runs. When organisations adopt multiple platforms, governance becomes fragmented.

Rahwan et al. (2019) argued that AI systems should be analysed as actors within broader ecosystems. Applying this reasoning architecturally implied that AI-enabled workflows required oversight mechanisms external to specific runtime implementations.

Operational AI literature increasingly used terms such as MLOps and LLM observability to describe lifecycle monitoring practices. These approaches emphasised logging, tracing, and evaluation. However, they frequently assumed single-platform deployment environments or centralised model-serving infrastructure. They rarely addressed low-code orchestration ecosystems common in marketing operations.

Furthermore, emerging discussions on agentic AI systems highlighted risks associated with autonomous task execution and tool access. Legal and regulatory commentary underscored that increased capability without corresponding oversight amplified operational exposure.

What remained insufficiently addressed in literature was how governance could be designed as a runtime-independent architectural layer capable of supervising heterogeneous execution engines.

This study therefore positioned governance not as a feature inside automation tools but as an externalised control layer. By decoupling governance from execution infrastructure, the architecture allowed consistent audit logging, monitoring, and approval enforcement across multiple platforms.

This design operationalised governance principles into system architecture.

2.3 Interoperability in Multi-Platform Marketing Automation Environments
The third objective involved implementing and evaluating a governed workflow across two automation platforms to demonstrate interoperability.

Contemporary organisations rarely operate within single-tool ecosystems. Tool heterogeneity results from cost considerations, feature differentiation, departmental autonomy, and legacy constraints. In marketing operations specifically, analytics tools, CRM systems, ad platforms, and reporting engines coexist with multiple automation tools.

Business Process Management research demonstrated that automation improved efficiency but required structured governance to avoid fragmentation. Inter-organisational process governance studies emphasised performance measurement and data consistency across distributed systems.

Workflow monitoring research historically identified audit trail data as central to process transparency. Yet most classical workflow management systems assumed unified execution engines rather than loosely coupled low-code ecosystems.

Low-code automation platforms lowered technical barriers and accelerated deployment. However, they often prioritised usability and integration convenience over enterprise-grade governance architecture. This created environments in which workflows executed reliably within a platform but lacked cross-platform traceability.

Recent industry analysis of agentic AI initiatives indicated that many projects stalled at pilot stage due to governance and observability weaknesses rather than lack of technical capability. This observation reinforced the importance of designing systems where oversight was foundational rather than reactive.

The literature therefore supported the necessity of demonstrating cross-platform interoperability governed by a consistent audit and monitoring layer. Implementing the weekly marketing reporting workflow across Make and n8n provided a realistic operational context for validating this architecture.

2.4 Evaluation of Transparency, Auditability, and Reliability
The fourth objective examined evaluation of the proposed platform in terms of transparency, auditability, and reliability.

Evaluation literature in AI deployment increasingly emphasised observability and lifecycle governance. LLM observability research formalised principles for tracing prompts, outputs, system calls, and errors across system lifecycles. These principles aligned directly with AgentOps concepts.

Human–AI interaction research emphasised that systems must communicate status, support error recovery, and provide intelligible behaviour explanations. In backend workflow environments, this translated into comprehensive logs, execution histories, and failure notifications.

Regulatory expectations further reinforced evaluation requirements. The EU AI Act included documentation and logging obligations for certain AI systems. Even where not legally mandated, these standards reflected emerging norms in AI accountability.

NIST AI RMF emphasised measurement and ongoing monitoring as continuous processes rather than one-time checks. Therefore, evaluation in this study focused not on model accuracy but on governance effectiveness.

Operational indicators included:

Presence of centralised execution logs

Consistency of audit records across runtimes

Traceability of workflow steps

Detectability of errors

Documentation of AI interactions

By grounding evaluation metrics in both governance standards and observability research, the study ensured that assessment criteria were theoretically justified and practically measurable.

2.5 Research Gap and Conceptual Framework
Although literature extensively addressed AI risk, observability, workflow management, and governance frameworks, three critical gaps were identified.

First, governance discussions remained largely policy-oriented rather than architecturally operationalised.

Second, AI observability research assumed unified runtime environments rather than heterogeneous low-code ecosystems.

Third, marketing automation literature focused on efficiency and performance outcomes rather than cross-platform governance design.

There was limited scholarship translating governance principles into a runtime-independent architectural pattern for AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

This study addressed that gap by designing and evaluating an AgentOps governance layer external to execution runtimes.

Conceptual Framework
Independent Variable
Implementation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Governance Layer

Dependent Variables

Execution Transparency

Audit Consistency

Workflow Reliability

Control Variables

Workflow type

Automation platforms used

Reporting schedule

Data sources

Expected Relationship
The implementation of a runtime-independent governance layer improved transparency, strengthened audit consistency, and enhanced workflow reliability across heterogeneous automation platforms.

This framework directly informed the system architecture described in Chapter One and the implementation methodology described in Chapter Three.

Clearly articulates research gaps

Critiques limitations of current literature

Aligns architecture with theory

Prepares directly for Chapter Three diagrams

CHAPTER THREE
METHODOLOGY
3.0 Introduction
This chapter described the methodology adopted in the design, development, and evaluation of the runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The methodology integrated principles from systems analysis and design, design science research, and structured system implementation. The chapter detailed the research design, system modelling techniques, data handling procedures, and ethical considerations.

3.1 Research Methodology
The study adopted a Design Science Research approach. Design Science Research focuses on the creation and evaluation of artefacts intended to solve identified organisational problems. In this study, the artefact was a runtime-independent AgentOps governance platform.

The methodology followed structured systems development phases:

Problem Identification

Requirement Analysis

System Design

Prototype Development

Testing and Evaluation

This approach was appropriate because the research did not aim to test an abstract theory. Instead, it aimed to design, implement, and evaluate a functional system.

3.2 System Requirements Analysis
System requirements were derived from:

• Identified governance challenges in Chapter Two
• Operational needs of a generic marketing operations environment
• Principles of AI observability and auditability

3.2.1 Functional Requirements
The system was required to:

Capture marketing workflow inputs.

Assign unique workflow identifiers.

Route tasks across automation platforms.

Log all execution steps in a centralized data store.

Record AI interactions including prompts and outputs.

Track workflow status changes.

Generate consolidated audit reports.

Detect and log workflow errors.

Produce weekly performance summaries.

3.2.2 Non-Functional Requirements
The system was required to:

• Maintain consistency across platforms
• Support scalability
• Ensure data traceability
• Provide execution transparency
• Minimize dependency on specific runtimes
• Operate within low-code constraints

The system was implemented using a layered architecture consisting of:

Layer 1: Input Layer
Layer 2: Orchestration Layer
Layer 3: Intelligence Layer
Layer 4: Data and Knowledge Layer
Layer 5: Output and Feedback Layer

Governance logic was embedded as a runtime-independent control mechanism that:

• Standardized logging format
• Assigned global workflow IDs
• Enforced approval requirements
• Recorded AI interactions
• Centralized monitoring

Automation runtimes executed workflow logic, but governance decisions and logs were controlled externally.

• [Make.com](http://Make.com) for orchestration testing
• n8n for interoperability validation
• Google Sheets as centralized audit and data repository
• OpenAI API for AI processing
• Looker Studio for dashboard reporting

Each workflow execution generated standardized log entries that were stored independently of the automation runtime.

Testing was conducted in three phases:

3.6.1 Functional Testing
Each module was tested individually to verify correct execution and logging.

3.6.2 Cross-Platform Consistency Testing
The same workflow was executed in both automation platforms. Logs were compared to ensure structural consistency.

3.6.3 Error Handling Testing
Intentional errors were introduced to verify detection, logging, and reporting mechanisms.

3.7 Evaluation Metrics
The system was evaluated using the following criteria:

Execution Transparency
Measured by traceability of workflow steps.

Audit Consistency
Measured by log standardization across platforms.

Workflow Reliability
Measured by successful completion rate and error detectability.

The study did not involve human subjects or sensitive personal data. All datasets used were simulated marketing data. API keys and system credentials were protected during development.

The system was designed to respect principles of accountability, transparency, and responsible AI usage.

CHAPTER THREE
RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND SYSTEM DESIGN
3.0 Introduction
This chapter presents the methodology adopted for the design, development, and evaluation of the runtime-independent AgentOps platform proposed in this research. The purpose of the system is to provide a governance and monitoring layer capable of supervising AI-enabled marketing workflows executed across heterogeneous automation platforms.

The chapter explains the research design, system requirements, system modelling techniques, architecture design, prototype development tools, testing procedures, and evaluation criteria used in the study. The methodology integrates principles from systems analysis and design with design science research to ensure that the resulting artefact addresses the governance challenges identified in Chapter Two.

The chapter is structured as follows. Section 3.1 describes the research methodology adopted in the study. Section 3.2 presents the system requirements analysis derived from the problem statement and research objectives. Section 3.3 introduces the system modelling techniques used to describe system structure and behaviour. Section 3.4 explains the proposed system architecture. Section 3.5 outlines the development environment and implementation tools used in the prototype. Section 3.6 describes the testing procedures used to validate the system. Section 3.7 defines the evaluation metrics used to assess system performance. Finally, Section 3.8 addresses ethical considerations associated with the research.

3.1 Research Methodology
The study adopted a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology. Design Science Research focuses on the development and evaluation of technological artefacts intended to solve identified organisational or operational problems (Hevner et al., 2004).

Unlike purely theoretical research approaches that aim to generate explanatory knowledge, Design Science Research focuses on producing practical artefacts such as models, frameworks, architectures, or systems. These artefacts are evaluated to determine whether they effectively address the problem under investigation.

In this research, the artefact being developed is a runtime-independent AgentOps governance platform designed to provide transparency, auditability, and reliability for AI-driven marketing workflows operating across different automation environments.

The Design Science Research process in this study followed five major stages.

3.1.1 Problem Identification
The first stage involved identifying governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-enabled automation workflows in marketing operations environments. As established in Chapter Two, organisations frequently deploy marketing workflows using multiple low-code automation platforms such as Make and n8n.

While these platforms simplify workflow orchestration and system integration, they often maintain isolated logging and monitoring environments. As a result, organisations face difficulty in achieving consistent oversight of workflow execution across multiple automation systems.

The lack of unified monitoring creates several operational risks. These include limited visibility into workflow behaviour, difficulty tracing AI-generated outputs, and challenges auditing system activity during failure or error conditions.

The problem therefore centres on the absence of a runtime-independent governance architecture capable of supervising workflows executed across heterogeneous automation environments.

3.1.2 Requirement Analysis
Following problem identification, system requirements were derived from the governance challenges identified in Chapter Two. These requirements defined the functional and operational capabilities that the proposed AgentOps platform must support.

Requirements analysis also ensured that the system design remained feasible within the scope of a prototype implementation while still demonstrating the architectural concept under investigation.

3.1.3 System Design
The third stage involved designing the structural and functional architecture of the system. System design included modelling system processes, defining system actors, specifying data flows, and designing system components.

Structured systems analysis techniques were used to describe system behaviour and interactions.

These techniques included:

Context diagrams

Data flow diagrams

Use case diagrams

These models helped clarify how the governance layer interacts with marketing users, automation runtimes, AI services, and data storage components.

3.1.4 Prototype Development
After designing the system architecture, a functional prototype was implemented using selected development tools. The prototype implemented a simplified marketing workflow designed to simulate real operational conditions.

The prototype allowed testing of cross-platform governance features by executing the same workflow across two automation platforms.

3.1.5 System Evaluation
The final stage involved evaluating the prototype system based on governance effectiveness. Evaluation focused on three primary criteria.

Transparency of workflow execution

Consistency of audit logs across automation platforms

Reliability of workflow execution and monitoring

These evaluation criteria were derived directly from the research objectives established in Chapter One.

3.2 System Requirements Analysis
System requirements describe the capabilities that the proposed system must provide in order to address the governance challenges identified in the research problem.

Requirements were derived from three sources:

Governance issues identified in the literature review

Operational characteristics of marketing automation workflows

Observability principles from AI system monitoring research

The requirements were categorized as functional requirements and non-functional requirements.

3.2.1 Functional Requirements
Functional requirements define the specific operations that the system must perform.

The proposed AgentOps governance platform must support the following core functions.

Campaign Intake and Workflow Initiation
The system must allow marketing users to submit campaign requests or workflow triggers. These requests represent the starting point of automated marketing processes such as content reporting, campaign performance tracking, or weekly analytics summaries.

Upon submission, the system must assign a unique workflow identifier to ensure that every execution instance can be traced.

Cross-Platform Workflow Execution
The governance system must support triggering workflows across multiple automation platforms. In the prototype environment, this involves executing a workflow using both Make and n8n automation runtimes.

The ability to trigger workflows across multiple platforms demonstrates the runtime independence of the governance layer.

Execution Monitoring
The system must track workflow execution events. These events include workflow start time, workflow completion time, error occurrences, and execution status updates.

Monitoring information must be recorded in a centralized repository independent of the automation platform used.

AI Interaction Logging
Since the workflow incorporates AI-generated summaries and analysis, the system must record AI prompts and responses generated during workflow execution.

This logging process ensures traceability and supports auditability of AI system behaviour.

Performance Reporting
The system must aggregate workflow execution results and generate consolidated reports. These reports summarize workflow performance metrics and AI-generated insights.

Error Detection and Logging
The system must detect failures or anomalies during workflow execution and record these events in the audit log.

This capability ensures that system administrators can identify operational issues and investigate workflow errors.

3.2.2 Non Functional Requirements
Non-functional requirements define the operational qualities of the system.

Transparency
The system must provide clear visibility into workflow execution steps.

Auditability
The system must record sufficient information to reconstruct workflow execution history.

Scalability
The architecture must allow extension to additional automation platforms in future implementations.

Platform Independence
The governance layer must operate independently of specific automation runtimes.

Reliability
The system must ensure that workflows execute consistently and that failures are detected and logged.

3.3 System Modelling
System modelling techniques were used to visually represent system processes and interactions. These models provide a structured understanding of system behaviour and data flow.

Three modelling techniques were used.

Context Diagram

Data Flow Diagram

Use Case Diagram

These modelling tools are commonly used in systems analysis to describe system boundaries, system processes, and user interactions.

3.3.1 Context Diagram
The Context Diagram provides a high-level view of the system and its interactions with external entities.

The entire AgentOps platform is represented as a single process interacting with external actors.

External entities interacting with the system include:

Marketing analysts who initiate workflows

Automation runtimes responsible for executing workflows

AI services responsible for generating content summaries

Reporting dashboards that visualize workflow results

Data storage systems that store logs and metrics

The context diagram establishes the system boundary and illustrates the flow of information between the governance platform and its environment.

3.3.2 Level One Data Flow Diagram
The Level One Data Flow Diagram decomposes the system into its major functional components.

The system consists of six primary processes.

Process One: Campaign Intake
This process receives campaign input data from marketing users and validates the input information.

Process Two: Workflow Orchestration
This process determines which automation platform will execute the workflow and initiates execution through the selected runtime.

Process Three: Execution Monitoring
This process collects workflow execution data from automation runtimes and records the information in a centralized audit repository.

Process Four: AI Interaction Logging
This process records all interactions between the workflow and the AI processing service.

Process Five: Data Aggregation
This process aggregates workflow execution data and prepares datasets for reporting.

Process Six: Report Generation
This process produces final reports summarizing marketing performance insights generated by the workflow.

3.4 System Architecture Design
The proposed AgentOps system follows a layered architecture.

Layered architecture improves maintainability, scalability, and system clarity by separating system responsibilities into distinct components.

The architecture consists of five layers.

Input Layer
This layer captures marketing campaign requests and workflow triggers submitted by users.

Orchestration Layer
This layer coordinates workflow execution and communicates with automation runtimes such as Make and n8n.

Intelligence Layer
This layer processes data using AI services to generate analytical summaries and marketing insights.

Data Layer
This layer stores workflow execution logs, AI interaction records, and performance metrics.

Output Layer
This layer generates reports and visual dashboards used by marketing teams to review campaign performance.

The governance layer operates across these components by enforcing standardized logging and monitoring procedures.

3.5 Prototype Development Environment
The prototype system was implemented using widely available automation and analytics tools.

The development environment included the following components.

Automation orchestration was implemented using Make and n8n.

Centralized data storage was implemented using Google Sheets.

AI content analysis was implemented using the OpenAI API.

Data visualization and reporting were implemented using Looker Studio.

These tools were selected because they represent realistic technologies commonly used in marketing automation environments.

3.6 System Testing
System testing was conducted to validate system functionality and evaluate the effectiveness of the governance architecture.

Three testing strategies were applied.

Functional Testing
Functional testing verified that each system component performed its intended operation.

Interoperability Testing
The same workflow was executed across both automation platforms to confirm that the governance system could monitor execution consistently.

Error Simulation Testing
Artificial errors were introduced during workflow execution to test the ability of the system to detect and log failures.

3.7 Evaluation Metrics
The system was evaluated using three key performance indicators.

Execution Transparency
Measured by the ability to trace workflow execution steps across the system.

Audit Consistency
Measured by the completeness and structure of audit logs produced by the system.

Workflow Reliability
Measured by successful workflow completion rates and system ability to detect errors.

3.8 Ethical Considerations
The research did not involve human participants or personal data.

All datasets used during prototype development were simulated marketing data.

System credentials and API keys were protected throughout development.

The research adhered to principles of responsible AI deployment, transparency, and accountability.

Corrected Citations for Chapter Three
3.1 Research Methodology
The study adopted a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology. Design Science Research focuses on the development and evaluation of technological artefacts intended to solve identified organisational or operational problems (Hevner et al., 2004). Unlike purely theoretical research approaches that aim to generate explanatory knowledge, Design Science Research focuses on producing practical artefacts such as models, frameworks, architectures, or systems. These artefacts are evaluated to determine whether they effectively address the problem under investigation (Peffers et al., 2007).

In this research, the artefact being developed is a runtime-independent AgentOps governance platform designed to provide transparency, auditability, and reliability for AI-driven marketing workflows operating across different automation environments.

3.2 System Requirements Analysis
Requirements analysis is a foundational activity in systems development and involves identifying the functions a system must perform in order to satisfy user needs and organisational objectives (Sommerville, 2016). Functional requirements describe the specific operations a system must perform, while non-functional requirements define the quality attributes that the system must exhibit such as reliability, scalability, and maintainability (Pressman & Maxim, 2020).

3.3 System Modelling
System modelling techniques were used to visually represent system processes and interactions. These models provide a structured understanding of system behaviour and data flow within the proposed architecture. Structured modelling tools such as context diagrams, data flow diagrams, and use case diagrams are widely used in systems analysis and design to describe system boundaries, user interactions, and information flow (Dennis et al., 2021).

3.3.1 Context Diagram
A context diagram represents the highest level abstraction of a system and shows the system as a single process interacting with external entities (Dennis et al., 2021). It is used to define system boundaries and clarify how information flows between the system and its environment.

3.3.2 Data Flow Diagram
Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) provide a structured representation of how data moves through a system and how it is processed and stored (Yourdon, 1989). Level 1 DFDs expand the system into major processes and data stores, enabling analysts to understand how information flows between components.

3.3.3 Use Case Diagram
Use case diagrams are commonly used in object-oriented analysis to represent the interactions between system actors and system functionality (Booch et al., 2007). They help identify system capabilities from the perspective of users and external systems.

3.4 System Architecture Design
The proposed system follows a layered architecture design. Layered architectures are widely used in software engineering because they separate system responsibilities into independent modules, improving maintainability and scalability (Bass et al., 2021). Separation of concerns allows governance logic, workflow orchestration, AI processing, and data storage to operate as independent components while still functioning as part of an integrated system.

3.6 System Testing
Software testing ensures that a system performs according to its design specifications and that errors or failures can be identified and corrected before deployment (Pressman & Maxim, 2020). Functional testing verifies that each system component performs its intended operation, while integration testing evaluates interactions between system components.

3.7 Evaluation Metrics
Evaluation metrics were derived from principles of AI system observability and governance. Observability involves monitoring system behaviour through logs, metrics, and traces in order to understand system performance and detect anomalies (NIST, 2023). These principles are particularly important in AI-enabled systems where decision-making processes must remain transparent and auditable.

References (APA 7)
These should appear in your References section at the end of the thesis.

PART 1
REBUILT CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW (Improved but aligned with your thesis)
2.0 Introduction
This chapter reviews existing literature relevant to the design and evaluation of governed AI automation systems in marketing operations environments. The purpose of this review is to establish the theoretical and technological foundations upon which the proposed runtime-independent AgentOps platform is built.

The chapter examines four main areas relevant to the research problem. These include the operational challenges associated with AI-enabled marketing workflows, governance and monitoring of artificial intelligence systems, interoperability in multi-platform automation environments, and architectural approaches for achieving transparency and reliability in automated systems.

The review also identifies the limitations of existing approaches and establishes the research gap addressed by this study. Although recent research has explored AI governance frameworks, machine learning observability, and automation technologies, limited work has focused on governance architectures capable of supervising AI-enabled workflows across heterogeneous automation platforms.

2.1 AI-Driven Marketing Automation
Marketing automation systems have become a central component of modern organisational operations. These systems allow organisations to automate repetitive marketing tasks such as campaign management, customer segmentation, data analysis, and performance reporting. By integrating marketing platforms, analytics tools, and data pipelines, organisations can significantly improve operational efficiency and marketing responsiveness.

Recent advances in artificial intelligence have further expanded the capabilities of marketing automation systems. AI models are increasingly used to generate marketing insights, produce campaign summaries, analyse audience behaviour, and recommend marketing strategies. These capabilities allow organisations to process large volumes of data and extract insights that would otherwise be difficult to generate manually (Davenport & Ronanki, 2018).

Despite these benefits, the integration of AI into operational workflows introduces new challenges. AI-generated outputs may influence organisational decisions, yet the underlying reasoning processes of AI models are often opaque. This lack of transparency creates governance concerns, particularly when AI outputs are embedded within automated systems that operate with limited human oversight.

Research has shown that AI systems may produce plausible but incorrect outputs, a phenomenon commonly referred to as hallucination in large language models (Bender et al., 2021). When such outputs are integrated into automated workflows, the potential for misinformation or operational errors increases.

Furthermore, organisations frequently deploy marketing automation workflows across multiple tools and platforms. This fragmentation makes it difficult to monitor system behaviour consistently. As a result, organisations often struggle to maintain visibility over how automated decisions are generated and executed.

These challenges highlight the need for structured governance mechanisms capable of supervising AI-enabled workflows in complex automation environments.

2.2 AI Governance and Observability
AI governance refers to the policies, processes, and technologies used to ensure that artificial intelligence systems operate responsibly, transparently, and reliably. Governance mechanisms aim to reduce risks associated with AI deployment while ensuring that systems remain accountable and auditable.

In recent years, several frameworks have emerged to guide organisations in managing AI-related risks. The National Institute of Standards and Technology introduced the AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) to help organisations identify and mitigate risks associated with artificial intelligence systems (NIST, 2023). Similarly, the ISO/IEC 42001 AI Management System Standard, published in 2024, provides organisations with structured approaches for governing AI system development and deployment.

These frameworks emphasise transparency, accountability, monitoring, and documentation. However, they primarily provide organisational guidance rather than architectural implementation strategies. While the frameworks define what organisations should do to govern AI systems, they provide limited guidance on how governance should be embedded within operational technology infrastructures.

In parallel with governance research, the concept of AI observability has emerged in the field of machine learning operations. Observability refers to the ability to understand system behaviour through logs, metrics, and execution traces. In AI systems, observability involves monitoring model inputs, outputs, system performance, and decision pathways (Breck et al., 2017).

Observability tools allow engineers to detect anomalies, trace errors, and monitor system health. However, these tools are typically designed for machine learning pipelines rather than complex organisational workflows that combine AI models with automation tools.

As organisations increasingly deploy AI capabilities within business automation systems, the need for integrated governance and observability solutions has become more evident.

2.3 Automation Platforms and Workflow Fragmentation
Automation platforms have become widely adopted across organisations seeking to integrate digital systems and streamline operations. Platforms such as Make, Zapier, and n8n allow users to create automated workflows that connect multiple software applications without requiring extensive programming knowledge.

Low-code automation tools have significantly reduced the technical barriers associated with building operational workflows. These platforms allow organisations to automate data transfers, trigger events across systems, and orchestrate business processes using visual workflow builders.

While low-code automation tools offer flexibility and accessibility, they also introduce operational complexity. Organisations often deploy multiple automation tools simultaneously due to differences in pricing models, integration capabilities, and technical requirements.

The use of multiple automation platforms can lead to fragmented workflow environments. Each platform maintains its own logging systems, monitoring dashboards, and execution histories. As a result, organisations may struggle to obtain a unified view of how workflows operate across different platforms.

Research in business process management highlights the importance of monitoring and auditing workflow activities in order to maintain process transparency and reliability (van der Aalst, 2016). Without consistent monitoring mechanisms, organisations face difficulties in identifying system failures, diagnosing errors, and ensuring accountability.

These challenges become even more significant when automation workflows incorporate artificial intelligence components whose outputs influence operational decisions.

2.4 AgentOps and Operational Governance of AI Systems
The rapid expansion of AI applications has led to the emergence of AgentOps, a discipline focused on the operational management of AI agents and automated decision systems. AgentOps extends traditional DevOps and MLOps practices by emphasising governance, monitoring, and lifecycle management of AI-driven agents operating within organisational environments.

AgentOps frameworks focus on several key operational capabilities. These include monitoring agent behaviour, recording interactions between agents and external systems, maintaining audit logs, and ensuring that AI systems operate within defined governance constraints.

Recent research highlights the importance of governance infrastructures capable of supervising AI systems that operate autonomously within business processes. As AI agents gain the ability to interact with tools, access data sources, and execute tasks, organisations require mechanisms for maintaining control over these systems.

The need for operational governance is particularly relevant in environments where AI agents interact with automation tools. Without structured governance mechanisms, organisations may lose visibility into how AI agents interact with external systems and how decisions are generated.

Although AgentOps has begun to emerge as a concept within industry practice, academic research examining its architectural implementation remains limited. Most discussions of AgentOps focus on operational principles rather than system design approaches.

This gap creates an opportunity for research exploring how governance architectures can be implemented to supervise AI-enabled workflows across heterogeneous automation environments.

2.5 Research Gap
The literature reviewed in this chapter demonstrates that significant progress has been made in several related areas including AI governance frameworks, machine learning observability, business process monitoring, and automation platforms.

However, three important gaps remain evident.

First, existing AI governance frameworks primarily focus on policy and organisational processes rather than system architecture design.

Second, observability tools developed for machine learning systems are typically designed for model pipelines rather than cross-platform automation environments.

Third, current research on marketing automation systems focuses on efficiency and campaign optimisation rather than governance and transparency of AI-enabled workflows.

Consequently, there is limited research examining how governance architectures can be designed to supervise AI-driven workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

This study addresses this gap by designing and evaluating a runtime-independent AgentOps platform capable of providing governance and monitoring across heterogeneous automation environments.

ABSTRACT
This study examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. As organisations increasingly adopted Artificial Intelligence to automate marketing analytics and reporting workflows, governance and monitoring challenges emerged due to fragmented execution across multiple automation platforms.
The study identified limitations in cross-platform visibility, audit consistency, and execution transparency within AI-driven marketing workflows. To address these challenges, a runtime-independent governance architecture was designed to operate as a control layer separate from specific automation runtimes. A functional prototype was developed and deployed to execute a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across two automation platforms.
The system was evaluated based on its ability to improve execution transparency, maintain consistent audit logs, and enhance workflow reliability across heterogeneous environments. The findings demonstrated that decoupling governance from execution infrastructure improved monitoring consistency and strengthened operational accountability.
This study contributes to the emerging field of Agent Operations by applying structured systems analysis and design principles to AI workflow governance in organisational contexts.

Keywords: AgentOps, AI governance, marketing automation, runtime independence, workflow monitoring, audit logging
