CHAPTER ONE
1	INTRODUCTION
This project examined the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The study focused on the operational challenges that emerged when Artificial Intelligence driven workflows were executed across multiple automation platforms without a unified governance mechanism. As organisations increasingly integrated AI into marketing analytics, reporting, and decision-support processes, the need for structured oversight, transparency, and cross-platform observability became more critical.
Marketing operations historically relied on manual data consolidation, spreadsheet-based reporting, and human interpretation of campaign metrics. Over time, the growth of digital advertising ecosystems, customer data platforms, and real-time analytics tools introduced greater complexity into marketing workflows. To manage this complexity, organisations adopted automation platforms capable of orchestrating multi-step processes involving data extraction, transformation, AI analysis, and reporting.
Although these platforms improved efficiency and reduced manual workload, they were primarily designed for execution rather than governance. Each automation environment maintained its own internal logs, triggers, and error-handling mechanisms. When AI-driven processes were layered into these workflows, oversight became fragmented. Monitoring was confined to individual platforms, audit trails were inconsistent, and accountability was difficult to centralise.
The absence of unified governance created operational blind spots. When failures occurred or outputs were questioned, teams were required to investigate across multiple systems independently. This approach increased operational risk and reduced organisational confidence in AI-generated outputs. Research on production AI systems has shown that weak monitoring structures and insufficient observability contribute to hidden technical debt and long-term reliability concerns (Sculley et al., 2015). Furthermore, scholarship on AI ethics and accountability has highlighted the risks of deploying automated decision-support systems without adequate oversight mechanisms (Bender et al., 2021; Rahwan et al., 2019).
Within this context, the concept of Agent Operations, commonly referred to as AgentOps, emerged as an operational discipline focused on governing AI agents in production environments. AgentOps extends principles from DevOps and MLOps by emphasising lifecycle management, monitoring, logging, and accountability for AI-driven systems. However, many existing implementations remained tied to specific platforms or execution environments.
This project therefore addressed the need for a runtime-independent governance layer capable of supervising AI-driven marketing workflows across heterogeneous automation platforms. By designing and evaluating such a platform, the study aimed to demonstrate how consistent audit logging, execution transparency, and cross-platform observability could be achieved without dependence on a single automation runtime.
1.1	Background of the Study
Contemporary organisations operated within highly dynamic marketing environments characterised by large volumes of structured and unstructured data. Marketing teams relied on performance metrics such as conversion rates, engagement statistics, return on advertising spend, and audience segmentation analytics. These metrics were derived from multiple digital channels including search advertising, social media platforms, email campaigns, and customer relationship management systems.
To manage this complexity, automation platforms were increasingly adopted to integrate and coordinate marketing processes. These platforms enabled organisations to design workflows that extracted data from external systems, transformed it into structured formats, applied AI-based analysis, and generated reports for managerial review. In many cases, Large Language Models and other AI systems were integrated into workflows to summarise performance insights, detect anomalies, and generate recommendations.
Within a generic contemporary marketing operations environment, it was common for organisations to use more than one automation platform concurrently. This situation occurred for several reasons, including cost considerations, legacy system compatibility, feature differences, and departmental preferences. For example, one team might use one platform for campaign reporting automation while another team used a different platform for customer segmentation workflows.
Although such environments enabled flexibility, they also introduced fragmentation. Each platform maintained separate execution histories, logging formats, and monitoring dashboards. AI-driven workflows executed within one platform were not inherently visible within another. Consequently, organisational oversight was distributed rather than centralised.
Research has emphasised that AI systems deployed in operational contexts require robust monitoring frameworks to ensure reliability and accountability (Amershi et al., 2019). When AI-generated outputs directly influence business decisions, insufficient transparency can reduce trust and increase the likelihood of undetected errors. Furthermore, studies on machine behaviour have argued that AI systems should be treated as actors within organisational ecosystems whose behaviour must be observed and governed systematically (Rahwan et al., 2019).
In marketing operations, AI-generated summaries and analytics frequently informed strategic decisions such as budget reallocation, targeting adjustments, and campaign optimisation. If AI-driven workflows malfunctioned, produced incomplete outputs, or accessed incorrect data sources, the consequences could extend beyond technical inconvenience to strategic misdirection.
Despite these risks, governance mechanisms often remained embedded within individual automation platforms rather than implemented at an organisational level. This meant that monitoring capabilities were limited to the specific environment in which the workflow was executed. Cross-platform observability required manual consolidation of logs and data, which was inefficient and prone to oversight gaps.
The background of this study was therefore grounded in the recognition that contemporary marketing operations environments required a unified governance approach for AI-driven workflows. Such an approach needed to operate independently of specific automation runtimes while maintaining consistent auditability and execution transparency.
1.2	Problem Statement
In contemporary marketing operations environments, AI-driven workflows were executed across multiple automation platforms without a unified governance framework. Each platform stored execution logs independently and provided monitoring tools limited to its own runtime environment.
This operational configuration produced several interrelated problems.
First, workflow execution history was fragmented. When stakeholders sought to trace how a report was generated or which data sources were accessed, they were required to inspect logs within each platform separately. There was no centralised audit trail capable of providing a consolidated execution narrative.
Second, monitoring was inconsistent across platforms. One platform might provide detailed logs and error notifications, while another might offer minimal observability. This inconsistency reduced the reliability of oversight and created uneven accountability structures.
Third, failure detection was reactive rather than proactive. If an AI-driven workflow produced incorrect or incomplete outputs, identification of the failure depended largely on human review rather than systematic cross-platform monitoring.
Fourth, governance policies such as approval workflows, access controls, and audit retention rules were implemented differently across platforms. This variability limited the organisation’s ability to enforce consistent standards.
These problems originated directly from the way marketing operations were currently handled. Workflows were implemented independently within each automation platform based on functional convenience rather than governance architecture. As a result, governance was an afterthought rather than a foundational design component.
The absence of a runtime-independent governance layer increased operational risk, reduced transparency, and constrained the responsible scaling of AI automation. There was therefore a need to design and evaluate a platform capable of providing unified monitoring, audit logging, and execution control across heterogeneous automation environments.
1.3	Objectives
1.3.1	General Objective
To design and evaluate a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment.
1.3.2	Specific Objectives
i.	To investigate governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.
ii.	To design a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture that separated governance control from workflow execution runtimes.
iii.	To develop and deploy a functional prototype that executed a governed weekly marketing reporting workflow across two automation platforms within the project implementation period.
iv.	To analyze the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving execution transparency, auditability, and reliability across both automation environments.
Each objective was specific, measurable through system artefacts and evaluation criteria, achievable within the defined project timeframe, relevant to contemporary AI governance challenges, and bounded by the undergraduate project duration.
1.4	Research Questions
i.	What governance and monitoring challenges were associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms?
ii.	How could a runtime-independent AgentOps architecture be designed to govern AI-driven workflows independently of specific automation runtimes?
iii.	How was a governed marketing reporting workflow implemented across two automation platforms using the proposed platform?
iv.	To what extent did the proposed platform improve execution transparency, audit consistency, and workflow reliability across both automation environments?
These research questions were derived directly from the specific objectives and guided both system design and evaluation phases.
1.5	Significance of the Study
The significance of this study was both theoretical and practical.
From a theoretical perspective, the study extended existing research on AI governance by operationalising governance principles within a systems design framework. While prior scholarship had identified risks associated with unmonitored AI systems (Bender et al., 2021; Sculley et al., 2015), fewer studies had translated these concerns into concrete architectural designs within automation ecosystems. By conceptualising and implementing a runtime-independent AgentOps platform, the study bridged conceptual governance discourse and practical systems engineering.
From a practical perspective, the study addressed a timely organisational challenge. As AI-driven workflows became embedded in marketing operations, organisations required structured mechanisms to ensure accountability and transparency. The proposed platform demonstrated how governance could be decoupled from execution infrastructure, thereby enabling consistent oversight even in multi-platform environments.
The project also provided an opportunity to integrate knowledge acquired throughout the undergraduate programme, including systems analysis, database design, software engineering principles, and automation technologies. It required independent investigation, structured design reasoning, and implementation discipline, thereby fulfilling the academic objectives outlined in the project guideline document 
1.6	Scope and Limitation of the Study
The study focused on the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform applied to a single AI-driven marketing workflow, specifically weekly performance reporting. The workflow involved data aggregation, AI-generated summarisation, and structured output generation.
The implementation environment included two automation platforms selected to represent heterogeneous runtimes. The system architecture, logging mechanisms, and governance controls were developed and tested within this defined context.
The study did not include enterprise-scale deployment across multiple departments, nor did it integrate additional automation platforms beyond the two selected environments. Long-term performance benchmarking, cost analysis, and large-scale user adoption studies were outside the scope of the project. Additionally, the study did not evaluate AI model accuracy or retraining processes, as its focus remained on governance and operational oversight rather than model optimisation.
These limitations were defined to ensure feasibility within the academic timeframe and structural requirements of the undergraduate project.
 
CHAPTER TWO
2	LITERATURE REVIEW
2.1	Introduction
This chapter critically reviewed literature relevant to the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The review was structured according to the specific objectives of the study and examined four key domains: AI governance in operational contexts, architectural separation of control and execution, interoperability across heterogeneous automation platforms, and evaluation of transparency and reliability in AI-enabled workflow systems.
Rather than describing literature in isolation, this chapter analysed areas of agreement, identified limitations in current approaches, and articulated the research gap addressed by this study. The review demonstrated that although governance frameworks, automation systems, and AI observability principles had been extensively discussed, there remained limited translation of these concepts into executable architectural designs for cross-platform marketing automation environments.
2.2	Governance and Monitoring Challenges in AI-Driven Marketing Workflows
The first objective examined governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.
Literature on AI system deployment consistently highlighted that risks did not arise solely from model design but from operational embedding. Bender et al. (2021) argued that large language systems could produce outputs that appeared coherent and authoritative while masking uncertainty or error. This phenomenon created accountability risks when such outputs were integrated into decision-making environments.
In marketing operations, AI-generated summaries, anomaly detection outputs, and performance interpretations directly influenced managerial actions. When AI systems were embedded within automated workflows, tracing output origin and execution logic became increasingly complex. Sculley et al. (2015) introduced the concept of hidden technical debt in machine learning systems, explaining how monitoring gaps, entangled dependencies, and configuration complexity accumulated long-term maintenance burdens.
However, while these studies identified risks, they primarily focused on model lifecycle and engineering debt rather than cross-platform workflow governance. They did not address the operational reality in which marketing teams used multiple low-code automation tools simultaneously.
Human–AI interaction research further emphasised transparency and observability as essential for trust and effective use (Amershi et al., 2019). Yet most guidelines focused on user-facing transparency rather than backend execution traceability across heterogeneous systems.
Governance frameworks such as the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and ISO/IEC 42001 emphasised organisational oversight, documentation, and lifecycle management. These frameworks provided high-level governance principles but did not prescribe concrete architectural patterns for ensuring consistent monitoring across multiple automation runtimes.
The literature therefore revealed a critical limitation: governance was discussed at policy and risk levels, while operational implementation details within multi-platform marketing automation environments remained underexplored.
This study responded directly to that gap.
2.3	Architectural Separation of Governance and Execution
The second objective involved designing a runtime-independent architecture that separated governance control from execution runtimes.
Systems engineering principles have long supported modular design and separation of concerns. Layered architectures reduce coupling, improve maintainability, and isolate faults. In workflow automation systems, orchestration engines typically handle task routing and execution logic, while data storage and presentation layers are managed separately.
However, governance has often been embedded within execution environments. In low-code automation platforms, monitoring dashboards and logs remain confined to the platform in which the workflow runs. When organisations adopt multiple platforms, governance becomes fragmented.
Rahwan et al. (2019) argued that AI systems should be analysed as actors within broader ecosystems. Applying this reasoning architecturally implied that AI-enabled workflows required oversight mechanisms external to specific runtime implementations.
Operational AI literature increasingly used terms such as MLOps and LLM observability to describe lifecycle monitoring practices. These approaches emphasised logging, tracing, and evaluation. However, they frequently assumed single-platform deployment environments or centralised model-serving infrastructure. They rarely addressed low-code orchestration ecosystems common in marketing operations.
Furthermore, emerging discussions on agentic AI systems highlighted risks associated with autonomous task execution and tool access. Legal and regulatory commentary underscored that increased capability without corresponding oversight amplified operational exposure.
What remained insufficiently addressed in literature was how governance could be designed as a runtime-independent architectural layer capable of supervising heterogeneous execution engines.
This study therefore positioned governance not as a feature inside automation tools but as an externalised control layer. By decoupling governance from execution infrastructure, the architecture allowed consistent audit logging, monitoring, and approval enforcement across multiple platforms.
This design operationalised governance principles into system architecture.
2.4	Interoperability in Multi-Platform Marketing Automation Environments
The third objective involved implementing and evaluating a governed workflow across two automation platforms to demonstrate interoperability.
Contemporary organisations rarely operate within single-tool ecosystems. Tool heterogeneity results from cost considerations, feature differentiation, departmental autonomy, and legacy constraints. In marketing operations specifically, analytics tools, CRM systems, ad platforms, and reporting engines coexist with multiple automation tools.
Business Process Management research demonstrated that automation improved efficiency but required structured governance to avoid fragmentation. Inter-organisational process governance studies emphasised performance measurement and data consistency across distributed systems.
Workflow monitoring research historically identified audit trail data as central to process transparency. Yet most classical workflow management systems assumed unified execution engines rather than loosely coupled low-code ecosystems.
Low-code automation platforms lowered technical barriers and accelerated deployment. However, they often prioritised usability and integration convenience over enterprise-grade governance architecture. This created environments in which workflows executed reliably within a platform but lacked cross-platform traceability.
Recent industry analysis of agentic AI initiatives indicated that many projects stalled at pilot stage due to governance and observability weaknesses rather than lack of technical capability. This observation reinforced the importance of designing systems where oversight was foundational rather than reactive.
The literature therefore supported the necessity of demonstrating cross-platform interoperability governed by a consistent audit and monitoring layer. Implementing the weekly marketing reporting workflow across Make and n8n provided a realistic operational context for validating this architecture.
2.5	Evaluation of Transparency, Auditability, and Reliability
The fourth objective examined evaluation of the proposed platform in terms of transparency, auditability, and reliability.
Evaluation literature in AI deployment increasingly emphasised observability and lifecycle governance. LLM observability research formalised principles for tracing prompts, outputs, system calls, and errors across system lifecycles. These principles aligned directly with AgentOps concepts.
Human–AI interaction research emphasised that systems must communicate status, support error recovery, and provide intelligible behaviour explanations. In backend workflow environments, this translated into comprehensive logs, execution histories, and failure notifications.
Regulatory expectations further reinforced evaluation requirements. The EU AI Act included documentation and logging obligations for certain AI systems. Even where not legally mandated, these standards reflected emerging norms in AI accountability.
NIST AI RMF emphasised measurement and ongoing monitoring as continuous processes rather than one-time checks. Therefore, evaluation in this study focused not on model accuracy but on governance effectiveness.
Operational indicators included:
•	Presence of centralised execution logs
•	Consistency of audit records across runtimes
•	Traceability of workflow steps
•	Detectability of errors
•	Documentation of AI interactions
By grounding evaluation metrics in both governance standards and observability research, the study ensured that assessment criteria were theoretically justified and practically measurable.
2.6	Research Gap and Conceptual Framework
The literature reviewed in this chapter demonstrates that significant progress has been made in several related areas including AI governance frameworks, machine learning observability, business process monitoring, and automation platforms.
However, three important gaps remain evident.
First, existing AI governance frameworks primarily focus on policy and organisational processes rather than system architecture design.
Second, observability tools developed for machine learning systems are typically designed for model pipelines rather than cross-platform automation environments.
Third, current research on marketing automation systems focuses on efficiency and campaign optimisation rather than governance and transparency of AI-enabled workflows.
Consequently, there is limited research examining how governance architectures can be designed to supervise AI-driven workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.
This study addresses this gap by designing and evaluating a runtime-independent AgentOps platform capable of providing governance and monitoring across heterogeneous automation environments.
2.6.1	Conceptual Framework
Independent Variable
Implementation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Governance Layer
Dependent Variables
1.	Execution Transparency
2.	Audit Consistency
3.	Workflow Reliability
Control Variables
•	Workflow type
•	Automation platforms used
•	Reporting schedule
•	Data sources
Expected Relationship
The implementation of a runtime-independent governance layer improved transparency, strengthened audit consistency, and enhanced workflow reliability across heterogeneous automation platforms.
 
CHAPTER THREE
3	RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND SYSTEM DESIGN
3.1	Introduction
This chapter presents the methodology adopted for the design, development, and evaluation of the runtime-independent AgentOps platform proposed in this research. The purpose of the system is to provide a governance and monitoring layer capable of supervising AI-enabled marketing workflows executed across heterogeneous automation platforms.
The chapter explains the research design, system requirements, system modelling techniques, architecture design, prototype development tools, testing procedures, and evaluation criteria used in the study. The methodology integrates principles from systems analysis and design with design science research to ensure that the resulting artefact addresses the governance challenges identified in Chapter Two.
The chapter is structured as follows. Section 3.1 describes the research methodology adopted in the study. Section 3.2 presents the system requirements analysis derived from the problem statement and research objectives. Section 3.3 introduces the system modelling techniques used to describe system structure and behaviour. Section 3.4 explains the proposed system architecture. Section 3.5 outlines the development environment and implementation tools used in the prototype. Section 3.6 describes the testing procedures used to validate the system. Section 3.7 defines the evaluation metrics used to assess system performance. Finally, Section 3.8 addresses ethical considerations associated with the research.
3.2	Research Methodology
The study adopted a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology. Design Science Research focuses on the development and evaluation of technological artefacts intended to solve identified organisational or operational problems (Hevner et al., 2004).
Unlike purely theoretical research approaches that aim to generate explanatory knowledge, Design Science Research focuses on producing practical artefacts such as models, frameworks, architectures, or systems. These artefacts are evaluated to determine whether they effectively address the problem under investigation.
In this research, the artefact being developed is a runtime-independent AgentOps governance platform designed to provide transparency, auditability, and reliability for AI-driven marketing workflows operating across different automation environments.
The Design Science Research process in this study followed five major stages.
3.2.1	Problem Identification
The first stage involved identifying governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-enabled automation workflows in marketing operations environments. As established in Chapter Two, organisations frequently deploy marketing workflows using multiple low-code automation platforms such as Make and n8n.
While these platforms simplify workflow orchestration and system integration, they often maintain isolated logging and monitoring environments. As a result, organisations face difficulty in achieving consistent oversight of workflow execution across multiple automation systems.
The lack of unified monitoring creates several operational risks. These include limited visibility into workflow behaviour, difficulty tracing AI-generated outputs, and challenges auditing system activity during failure or error conditions.
The problem therefore centres on the absence of a runtime-independent governance architecture capable of supervising workflows executed across heterogeneous automation environments.
3.2.2	Requirement Analysis
Following problem identification, system requirements were derived from the governance challenges identified in Chapter Two. These requirements defined the functional and operational capabilities that the proposed AgentOps platform must support.
Requirements analysis also ensured that the system design remained feasible within the scope of a prototype implementation while still demonstrating the architectural concept under investigation.
3.2.3	System Design
The third stage involved designing the structural and functional architecture of the system. System design included modelling system processes, defining system actors, specifying data flows, and designing system components.
Structured systems analysis techniques were used to describe system behaviour and interactions.
These techniques included:
•	Context diagrams
•	Data flow diagrams
•	Use case diagrams
These models helped clarify how the governance layer interacts with marketing users, automation runtimes, AI services, and data storage components.
3.2.4	Prototype Development
After designing the system architecture, a functional prototype was implemented using selected development tools. The prototype implemented a simplified marketing workflow designed to simulate real operational conditions.
The prototype allowed testing of cross-platform governance features by executing the same workflow across two automation platforms.
3.2.5	System Evaluation
The final stage involved evaluating the prototype system based on governance effectiveness. Evaluation focused on three primary criteria.
1.	Transparency of workflow execution
2.	Consistency of audit logs across automation platforms
3.	Reliability of workflow execution and monitoring
These evaluation criteria were derived directly from the research objectives established in Chapter One.
3.3	System Requirements Analysis
System requirements describe the capabilities that the proposed system must provide in order to address the governance challenges identified in the research problem.
Requirements were derived from three sources:
1.	Governance issues identified in the literature review
2.	Operational characteristics of marketing automation workflows
3.	Observability principles from AI system monitoring research
The requirements were categorized as functional requirements and non-functional requirements.
3.3.1	Functional Requirements
Functional requirements define the specific operations that the system must perform.
The proposed AgentOps governance platform must support the following core functions.
3.3.1.1	Campaign Intake and Workflow Initiation
The system must allow marketing users to submit campaign requests or workflow triggers. These requests represent the starting point of automated marketing processes such as content reporting, campaign performance tracking, or weekly analytics summaries.
Upon submission, the system must assign a unique workflow identifier to ensure that every execution instance can be traced.
3.3.1.2	Cross-Platform Workflow Execution
The governance system must support triggering workflows across multiple automation platforms. In the prototype environment, this involves executing a workflow using both Make and n8n automation runtimes.
The ability to trigger workflows across multiple platforms demonstrates the runtime independence of the governance layer.
3.3.1.3	Execution Monitoring
The system must track workflow execution events. These events include workflow start time, workflow completion time, error occurrences, and execution status updates.
Monitoring information must be recorded in a centralized repository independent of the automation platform used.
3.3.1.4	AI Interaction Logging
Since the workflow incorporates AI-generated summaries and analysis, the system must record AI prompts and responses generated during workflow execution.
This logging process ensures traceability and supports auditability of AI system behaviour.
3.3.1.5	Performance Reporting
The system must aggregate workflow execution results and generate consolidated reports. These reports summarize workflow performance metrics and AI-generated insights.
3.3.1.6	Error Detection and Logging
The system must detect failures or anomalies during workflow execution and record these events in the audit log.
This capability ensures that system administrators can identify operational issues and investigate workflow errors.
3.3.2	Non Functional Requirements
Non-functional requirements define the operational qualities of the system.
3.3.2.1	Transparency
The system must provide clear visibility into workflow execution steps.
3.3.2.2	Auditability
The system must record sufficient information to reconstruct workflow execution history.
3.3.2.3	Scalability
The architecture must allow extension to additional automation platforms in future implementations.
3.3.2.4	Platform Independence
The governance layer must operate independently of specific automation runtimes.
3.3.2.5	Reliability
The system must ensure that workflows execute consistently and that failures are detected and logged.
3.4	System Modelling
System modelling techniques were used to visually represent system processes and interactions. These models provide a structured understanding of system behaviour and data flow.
Three modelling techniques were used.
1.	Context Diagram
2.	Data Flow Diagram
3.	Use Case Diagram
These modelling tools are commonly used in systems analysis to describe system boundaries, system processes, and user interactions.
3.4.1	Context Diagram
The Context Diagram provides a high-level view of the system and its interactions with external entities.
The entire AgentOps platform is represented as a single process interacting with external actors.
External entities interacting with the system include:
•	Marketing analysts who initiate workflows
•	Automation runtimes responsible for executing workflows
•	AI services responsible for generating content summaries
•	Reporting dashboards that visualize workflow results
•	Data storage systems that store logs and metrics
The context diagram establishes the system boundary and illustrates the flow of information between the governance platform and its environment.
Figure 1 presents the context diagram of the proposed runtime-independent AgentOps platform and shows its interaction with external entities within a contemporary marketing operations environment.
 
Figure 1: Context Diagram of the Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform
The context diagram showed that the AgentOps platform functioned as the central governance layer between marketing users, automation runtimes, AI services, reporting tools, and data storage systems. This confirmed that governance and monitoring were externalised from the workflow execution environments.
3.4.2	Level One Data Flow Diagram
The Level One Data Flow Diagram decomposes the system into its major functional components.
The system consists of six primary processes.
3.4.2.1	Process One: Campaign Intake
This process receives campaign input data from marketing users and validates the input information.
3.4.2.2	Process Two: Workflow Orchestration
This process determines which automation platform will execute the workflow and initiates execution through the selected runtime.
3.4.2.3	Process Three: Execution Monitoring
This process collects workflow execution data from automation runtimes and records the information in a centralized audit repository.
3.4.2.4	Process Four: AI Interaction Logging
This process records all interactions between the workflow and the AI processing service.
3.4.2.5	Process Five: Data Aggregation
This process aggregates workflow execution data and prepares datasets for reporting.
3.4.2.6	Process Six: Report Generation
This process produces final reports summarizing marketing performance insights generated by the workflow.
Figure 2 presents the Level 1 Data Flow Diagram of the proposed system and illustrates the major processes, data stores, and data flows involved in workflow governance and monitoring.
 
Figure 2: Level 1 Data Flow Diagram of the Proposed AgentOps Platform
The Level 1 Data Flow Diagram illustrated the internal logic of the proposed platform. It showed how workflow requests were received, executed, monitored, logged, and transformed into reports. It also demonstrated how AI interactions and audit records were captured in separate but related data stores to improve traceability.
3.5	System Architecture Design
The proposed AgentOps system follows a layered architecture.
Layered architecture improves maintainability, scalability, and system clarity by separating system responsibilities into distinct components.
The architecture consists of five layers.
3.5.1.1	Input Layer
This layer captures marketing campaign requests and workflow triggers submitted by users.
3.5.1.2	Orchestration Layer
This layer coordinates workflow execution and communicates with automation runtimes such as Make and n8n.
3.5.1.3	Intelligence Layer
This layer processes data using AI services to generate analytical summaries and marketing insights.
3.5.1.4	Data Layer
This layer stores workflow execution logs, AI interaction records, and performance metrics.
3.5.1.5	Output Layer
This layer generates reports and visual dashboards used by marketing teams to review campaign performance.
The governance layer operates across these components by enforcing standardized logging and monitoring procedures.
Figure 3 presents the use case diagram of the proposed system and identifies the primary actors and their interactions with the AgentOps platform.
 
Figure 3: Use Case Diagram of the Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform
The use case diagram identified the main actors interacting with the system and clarified their roles in workflow initiation, approval, execution monitoring, reporting, and error investigation. This supported the system’s governance objectives by showing how accountability was distributed across users, runtimes, and AI services.
3.6	Prototype Development Environment
The prototype system was implemented using widely available automation and analytics tools.
The development environment included the following components.
Automation orchestration was implemented using Make and n8n.
Centralized data storage was implemented using Google Sheets.
AI content analysis was implemented using the OpenAI API.
Data visualization and reporting were implemented using Looker Studio.
These tools were selected because they represent realistic technologies commonly used in marketing automation environments.
3.7	System Testing
System testing was conducted to validate system functionality and evaluate the effectiveness of the governance architecture.
Three testing strategies were applied.
3.7.1.1	Functional Testing
Functional testing verified that each system component performed its intended operation.
3.7.1.2	Interoperability Testing
The same workflow was executed across both automation platforms to confirm that the governance system could monitor execution consistently.
3.7.1.3	Error Simulation Testing
Artificial errors were introduced during workflow execution to test the ability of the system to detect and log failures.
3.8	Evaluation Metrics
The system was evaluated using three key performance indicators.
3.8.1.1	Execution Transparency
Measured by the ability to trace workflow execution steps across the system.
3.8.1.2	Audit Consistency
Measured by the completeness and structure of audit logs produced by the system.
3.8.1.3	Workflow Reliability
Measured by successful workflow completion rates and system ability to detect errors.
3.9	Ethical Considerations
The research did not involve human participants or personal data.
All datasets used during prototype development were simulated marketing data.
System credentials and API keys were protected throughout development.
The research adhered to principles of responsible AI deployment, transparency, and accountability.

CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW
2.1 Introduction
This chapter critically reviewed literature relevant to the design and evaluation of a runtime-independent AgentOps platform for governed AI automation in a contemporary marketing operations environment. The review was structured according to the specific objectives of the study and examined four key domains: governance and monitoring challenges in AI-driven operational systems, architectural separation of governance and execution infrastructure, interoperability within heterogeneous automation ecosystems, and evaluation of transparency and reliability in AI-enabled workflow environments.

The literature review served two main purposes. First, it established the theoretical foundations required to understand governance challenges associated with AI-enabled automation systems. Second, it examined existing research in order to identify limitations in current approaches and clarify the research gap addressed by this study.

Contemporary organisations increasingly deploy Artificial Intelligence systems within operational environments rather than purely analytical contexts. AI models are now integrated into production workflows where they process real-time data, generate reports, support decision-making, and interact with organisational systems. These developments have led to increased interest in governance, monitoring, and lifecycle management of AI-enabled systems (Amershi et al., 2019; NIST, 2023).

However, much of the existing research has focused either on algorithmic performance or on policy-level governance frameworks. Less attention has been given to the architectural implementation of governance mechanisms in operational environments where workflows span multiple automation platforms.

The review therefore analysed how existing literature addresses governance challenges, architectural design principles, interoperability constraints, and evaluation approaches relevant to AI-driven marketing workflows. By synthesising insights across these areas, the chapter established the conceptual foundation for the runtime-independent AgentOps architecture proposed in this study.

2.2 Governance and Monitoring Challenges in AI-Driven Marketing Workflows
The first objective of the study examined governance and monitoring challenges associated with AI-driven marketing workflows executed across multiple automation platforms.

Artificial Intelligence systems have become increasingly embedded in operational environments where their outputs influence organisational decisions. Marketing operations in particular have experienced rapid adoption of AI-driven tools for analytics, campaign optimisation, customer segmentation, and content generation (Davenport & Ronanki, 2018). These systems allow organisations to process large volumes of marketing data and extract actionable insights at unprecedented speed.

However, the integration of AI systems into operational workflows introduces new governance challenges. Unlike traditional software systems that follow deterministic rules, many AI models produce probabilistic outputs whose reasoning processes may not be directly interpretable. This characteristic has raised concerns regarding accountability, transparency, and reliability of AI-generated outputs.

Bender et al. (2021) argued that large language models often generate responses that appear coherent and authoritative even when they are incorrect or unsupported by evidence. This phenomenon presents governance risks when AI-generated outputs are incorporated into automated workflows that influence organisational decision-making. In marketing environments, AI-generated summaries may guide strategic decisions such as advertising budget allocation or campaign adjustments. If these outputs are inaccurate or based on incomplete data, the consequences may extend beyond technical errors to strategic misdirection.

Operational challenges are further compounded when AI systems are embedded within complex software infrastructures. Sculley et al. (2015) introduced the concept of hidden technical debt in machine learning systems, explaining that production AI environments often accumulate complexity through entangled dependencies, insufficient monitoring, and poorly documented workflows. Over time, these conditions make systems increasingly difficult to maintain and govern.

In marketing operations environments, workflows frequently involve multiple interconnected systems including advertising platforms, analytics tools, customer relationship management systems, and reporting dashboards. Automation platforms are used to orchestrate these interactions by coordinating data extraction, transformation, analysis, and reporting tasks. While these platforms improve efficiency, they also increase system complexity by introducing additional layers of automation.

Human–AI interaction research has highlighted the importance of transparency and explainability when AI systems operate in decision-support roles (Amershi et al., 2019). Transparency enables users to understand how outputs are generated and provides mechanisms for identifying errors or inconsistencies. However, many automation platforms provide limited visibility into internal workflow execution processes. Logs may capture system events but often fail to present comprehensive execution narratives that link data sources, AI interactions, and final outputs.

These limitations become more pronounced in multi-platform environments. Organisations often deploy multiple automation tools simultaneously due to differences in integration capabilities, departmental preferences, and cost considerations. Each platform maintains its own monitoring interface and logging mechanisms. Consequently, governance becomes fragmented across separate execution environments.

The fragmentation of monitoring systems introduces several operational risks. First, stakeholders may struggle to trace the origin of AI-generated outputs when data passes through multiple automation platforms. Second, failure detection becomes inconsistent because monitoring tools are limited to individual execution environments. Third, audit trails become incomplete when logs are distributed across multiple systems without centralised consolidation.

Governance frameworks have begun to address these issues at the organisational level. The National Institute of Standards and Technology introduced the Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework to guide organisations in identifying and mitigating risks associated with AI deployment (NIST, 2023). Similarly, emerging international standards such as ISO/IEC 42001 provide guidance on managing AI systems throughout their lifecycle.

These frameworks emphasise documentation, transparency, accountability, and continuous monitoring. However, they primarily describe governance principles rather than specific architectural implementations. They do not prescribe concrete system designs capable of enforcing governance policies across heterogeneous automation environments.

As a result, organisations attempting to operationalise AI governance often rely on ad hoc monitoring practices implemented within individual platforms. These approaches lack consistency and fail to provide unified oversight of AI-enabled workflows.

The literature therefore indicates that while governance risks associated with AI deployment are widely recognised, the architectural mechanisms required to implement governance in multi-platform automation environments remain insufficiently explored.

2.3 Architectural Separation of Governance and Execution
The second objective of the study involved designing a runtime-independent architecture that separates governance control from workflow execution runtimes.

Software engineering research has long emphasised the importance of modular design and separation of concerns in complex systems. Modular architectures allow different system components to operate independently while maintaining defined communication interfaces. This approach reduces system complexity, improves maintainability, and facilitates scalability (Bass et al., 2021).

Layered architecture is one of the most widely used design patterns in enterprise software systems. In layered architectures, system responsibilities are divided into distinct functional layers such as presentation, business logic, data management, and integration services. Each layer performs specific tasks while interacting with adjacent layers through well-defined interfaces.

The principle of separation of concerns suggests that governance mechanisms should be implemented independently from execution infrastructure. When governance logic is embedded directly within execution platforms, monitoring capabilities become tightly coupled with specific runtime environments. This coupling reduces flexibility and complicates system oversight when multiple platforms are used simultaneously.

Research on machine behaviour has suggested that AI systems should be analysed as actors within broader socio-technical ecosystems (Rahwan et al., 2019). From an architectural perspective, this implies that AI-driven systems require external oversight mechanisms capable of observing and regulating system behaviour across organisational environments.

Operational practices such as DevOps and MLOps have attempted to address these challenges by introducing monitoring and lifecycle management practices for software and machine learning systems. DevOps practices emphasise continuous integration, automated testing, and deployment pipelines, while MLOps extends these practices to include model training, evaluation, and monitoring (Breck et al., 2017).

However, many MLOps implementations assume centralised model-serving environments where models are deployed through unified infrastructure. Marketing automation ecosystems rarely follow this architecture. Instead, workflows are distributed across multiple low-code automation platforms that orchestrate interactions between external services.

This operational reality creates governance challenges that differ from those addressed by traditional MLOps frameworks. In particular, monitoring capabilities must extend beyond individual platforms to capture workflow behaviour across heterogeneous execution environments.

Recent discussions within the emerging field of Agent Operations, commonly referred to as AgentOps, have begun to address these challenges. AgentOps focuses on operational governance of AI agents that interact with tools, data sources, and external systems. The discipline extends principles from DevOps and MLOps by emphasising accountability, monitoring, logging, and lifecycle management of AI-driven agents operating within production environments.

Despite increasing interest in AgentOps practices, most implementations remain platform-specific. Monitoring systems are typically integrated directly into the platforms where AI agents operate. As a result, governance capabilities remain tied to specific runtime environments.

The literature therefore suggests that a more flexible architectural approach is required. Instead of embedding governance inside automation platforms, governance mechanisms can be externalised into a runtime-independent control layer capable of supervising workflows executed across multiple platforms.

Such an architecture aligns with broader principles of distributed system design, where centralised monitoring services observe system behaviour across multiple execution nodes. By decoupling governance from execution infrastructure, organisations can maintain consistent oversight regardless of which automation platform performs the workflow.

The runtime-independent architecture proposed in this study operationalises this concept by introducing an AgentOps governance layer that supervises AI-driven workflows independently of the automation runtimes used to execute them.

2.4 Interoperability in Multi-Platform Marketing Automation Environments
The third objective of the study involved implementing and evaluating a governed workflow across two automation platforms in order to demonstrate interoperability within heterogeneous automation environments.

Modern organisational information systems rarely operate within single-platform ecosystems. Instead, organisations adopt a variety of specialised tools that address different operational needs. In marketing operations, these tools may include advertising management platforms, analytics services, customer relationship management systems, and reporting tools.

Automation platforms such as Make and n8n play a central role in connecting these systems by orchestrating data flows and triggering automated processes. These platforms allow organisations to design workflows that integrate multiple applications without requiring extensive programming expertise.

Low-code automation technologies have significantly lowered the technical barriers associated with building and deploying operational workflows. However, they have also introduced new governance challenges. Each automation platform maintains its own execution environment, logging mechanisms, and monitoring dashboards. When organisations deploy multiple automation tools simultaneously, operational visibility becomes fragmented.

Research in business process management has emphasised the importance of monitoring and auditing workflow activities to ensure transparency and reliability (van der Aalst, 2016). Process mining techniques have been developed to analyse workflow execution logs and reconstruct system behaviour. However, many of these techniques assume unified workflow management systems that capture all execution events within a single platform.

In multi-platform automation environments, execution logs are distributed across different systems. Each platform records events using its own data structures and logging conventions. Consolidating this information into a unified execution narrative requires additional integration mechanisms.

The lack of interoperability between monitoring systems creates operational blind spots. Organisations may struggle to determine which platform executed specific workflow steps, how data was transformed during processing, and whether AI services generated outputs based on correct inputs.

Industry observations of AI adoption initiatives have revealed that governance and observability challenges frequently limit the scalability of automation projects. While organisations may successfully deploy AI-powered workflows in isolated contexts, scaling these systems across departments often exposes weaknesses in monitoring infrastructure and auditability mechanisms.

These observations highlight the importance of designing systems that prioritise interoperability and unified oversight from the outset. By demonstrating how a runtime-independent governance layer can supervise workflows executed across two different automation platforms, this study provides a practical illustration of how interoperability challenges can be addressed.

2.5 Evaluation of Transparency, Auditability, and Reliability
The fourth objective of the study involved evaluating the effectiveness of the proposed platform in improving transparency, audit consistency, and workflow reliability.

Evaluation frameworks for AI-enabled systems have increasingly emphasised the importance of observability and lifecycle governance. Observability refers to the ability to understand system behaviour through monitoring data such as logs, metrics, and execution traces. In distributed systems, observability enables engineers to diagnose failures, identify performance bottlenecks, and reconstruct system behaviour during incident investigations.

In AI-enabled workflows, observability extends beyond traditional system monitoring to include tracking of model inputs, outputs, prompts, and decision pathways. LLM observability research emphasises the importance of capturing interactions between AI models and external systems in order to ensure transparency and accountability.

Human–AI interaction research also emphasises system intelligibility as a key factor influencing trust in AI systems (Amershi et al., 2019). Systems that provide clear explanations of their behaviour allow users to detect anomalies, interpret outputs, and maintain confidence in automated processes.

Regulatory developments have further reinforced the importance of transparency and auditability in AI systems. Emerging regulatory frameworks such as the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act require organisations to maintain documentation and logging mechanisms that enable traceability of AI system behaviour.

Similarly, the NIST AI Risk Management Framework emphasises continuous monitoring as a critical component of responsible AI deployment. According to the framework, organisations must establish mechanisms for detecting system failures, evaluating performance, and ensuring accountability throughout the AI system lifecycle (NIST, 2023).

In the context of marketing automation workflows, transparency and auditability can be evaluated through several operational indicators. These include the presence of centralised execution logs, the ability to trace workflow steps across systems, the completeness of audit records, and the detectability of workflow failures.

Reliability can be assessed by examining whether workflows execute successfully across different automation platforms and whether monitoring systems accurately capture execution events. Systems that provide consistent monitoring across heterogeneous environments are better positioned to support organisational accountability and operational resilience.

By grounding evaluation metrics in both governance standards and observability research, this study ensures that system assessment criteria are both theoretically justified and practically measurable.

2.6 Research Gap and Conceptual Framework
The literature reviewed in this chapter demonstrates that significant progress has been made in several related areas including AI governance frameworks, machine learning observability, business process monitoring, and automation platforms.

However, several important limitations remain evident.

First, existing AI governance frameworks primarily focus on organisational policies and risk management processes rather than system architecture design. While frameworks such as the NIST AI Risk Management Framework provide guidance on responsible AI deployment, they do not specify architectural mechanisms capable of enforcing governance across distributed automation environments.

Second, observability tools developed for machine learning systems are typically designed for model pipelines rather than cross-platform workflow orchestration environments. These tools monitor model performance but do not capture the full execution context of AI-enabled workflows involving multiple external systems.

Third, research on marketing automation systems has focused largely on performance optimisation and campaign efficiency. Relatively little attention has been given to governance, transparency, and auditability of AI-enabled workflows operating within marketing operations environments.

These limitations highlight a clear research gap. While governance principles and monitoring techniques are widely discussed, there is limited research examining how governance architectures can be designed to supervise AI-driven workflows executed across heterogeneous automation platforms.

This study addresses this gap by designing and evaluating a runtime-independent AgentOps platform capable of providing unified monitoring, audit logging, and governance control across multiple automation runtimes.

2.6.1 Conceptual Framework
The conceptual framework guiding this study identifies the relationship between the implementation of a runtime-independent governance architecture and improvements in workflow monitoring outcomes.

Independent Variable
Implementation of a Runtime-Independent AgentOps Governance Layer

Dependent Variables
Execution Transparency
Audit Consistency
Workflow Reliability

Control Variables
Workflow type
Automation platforms used
Reporting schedule
Data sources

Expected Relationship
The implementation of a runtime-independent governance layer is expected to improve transparency, strengthen audit consistency, and enhance workflow reliability across heterogeneous automation platforms.

The conceptual framework therefore illustrates how architectural design choices influence operational governance outcomes within AI-driven marketing workflows.

CHAPTER THREE
RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND SYSTEM DESIGN
3.1 Introduction
This chapter presents the methodology adopted for the design, development, and evaluation of the runtime-independent AgentOps platform proposed in this study. The purpose of the system was to provide a governance and monitoring layer capable of supervising AI-driven marketing workflows executed across heterogeneous automation platforms. In line with the problem established in Chapter One and the research gap identified in Chapter Two, the methodology was designed to ensure that the resulting system addressed challenges of fragmented monitoring, inconsistent audit records, and weak operational visibility in AI-enabled workflow environments.

The chapter explains the research design, system requirements analysis, modelling techniques, architectural design, prototype development environment, testing strategy, evaluation criteria, and ethical considerations that guided the study. The methodology integrates principles from systems analysis and design with Design Science Research in order to produce a practical artefact grounded in a real organisational problem domain (Hevner et al., 2004; Peffers et al., 2007).

The chapter is organised as follows. Section 3.2 presents the research methodology adopted in the study. Section 3.3 outlines the system requirements analysis. Section 3.4 presents the system modelling techniques used to describe system structure and behaviour. Section 3.5 explains the proposed system architecture design. Section 3.6 describes the prototype development environment. Section 3.7 presents the testing strategy. Section 3.8 defines the evaluation metrics used to assess the performance of the system. Finally, Section 3.9 addresses ethical considerations relevant to the research.

3.2 Research Methodology
The study adopted a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology. Design Science Research is appropriate where the aim of a study is to create and evaluate an artefact intended to solve an identified problem in an information systems context (Hevner et al., 2004). In this research, the artefact was a runtime-independent AgentOps platform designed to improve governance, observability, and auditability of AI-driven marketing workflows operating across more than one automation platform.

Unlike purely explanatory research approaches that focus on theory testing, Design Science Research emphasises practical problem solving through the design, implementation, and evaluation of artefacts such as models, methods, architectures, and systems (Peffers et al., 2007). This made it suitable for the present study because the research objective was not simply to discuss AI governance conceptually, but to design and evaluate a working system that addressed a clear operational problem.

The Design Science Research process in this study followed five major stages:

Problem Identification

Requirement Analysis

System Design

Prototype Development

System Evaluation

Each stage is discussed below.

3.2.1 Problem Identification
The first stage involved identifying the specific governance and monitoring problem addressed by the study. As established in Chapter One, AI-driven marketing workflows in contemporary organisations were frequently executed across multiple automation platforms without a central governance layer. This resulted in fragmented execution histories, inconsistent logs, and reduced ability to trace AI-generated outputs across environments.

The problem was not the absence of automation tools. Rather, it was the absence of a runtime-independent mechanism capable of supervising workflows consistently across those tools. This distinction was important because it shaped the direction of the study. The purpose was not to replace existing platforms such as Make or n8n, but to design a governance layer that could sit above them and provide standardised monitoring, logging, and audit control.

3.2.2 Requirement Analysis
Following problem identification, system requirements were derived from the governance challenges identified in the literature review and the operational characteristics of a contemporary marketing operations environment. Requirements analysis was necessary to translate the research problem into system capabilities that could be designed, implemented, and evaluated.

Requirements were derived from three sources:

Governance issues identified in the literature review

Operational requirements of AI-driven marketing workflows

Observability and accountability principles drawn from AI governance frameworks

This stage ensured that the proposed system remained focused, feasible, and aligned with the study objectives.

3.2.3 System Design
The third stage involved designing the structure and behaviour of the proposed platform. This included identifying the core system components, defining their relationships, and representing system behaviour using structured analysis tools.

The study used system modelling techniques commonly applied in systems analysis and design, namely:

Context diagrams

Data flow diagrams

Use case diagrams

These techniques were appropriate because they helped clarify system boundaries, process logic, data movement, and actor interactions (Dennis et al., 2021; Yourdon, 1989; Booch et al., 2007).

3.2.4 Prototype Development
After system design, a functional prototype was developed using tools that reflected realistic automation practice in contemporary marketing operations. The prototype implemented a single governed workflow, weekly marketing performance reporting, across two automation platforms. This allowed the study to demonstrate runtime independence while remaining within the scope of an undergraduate project.

3.2.5 System Evaluation
The final stage involved evaluating the system against the governance outcomes identified in the research objectives. The evaluation focused on transparency, audit consistency, and workflow reliability. These criteria were selected because they directly reflected the problem established in Chapter One and the gaps identified in Chapter Two.

3.3 System Requirements Analysis
System requirements describe the capabilities that the proposed platform had to provide in order to address the governance challenges identified in the research problem. In software engineering, requirement analysis is a foundational activity because it connects user needs and organisational goals to concrete system functions and quality attributes (Sommerville, 2016; Pressman & Maxim, 2020).

The requirements for the runtime-independent AgentOps platform were divided into two categories:

Functional requirements

Non-functional requirements

3.3.1 Functional Requirements
Functional requirements define what the system must do. In this study, the functional requirements were derived from the governance problem, the selected marketing workflow, and the need for cross-platform execution control.

3.3.1.1 Campaign Intake and Workflow Initiation
The system had to allow a marketing user to initiate a governed workflow by submitting campaign or reporting input data. This was necessary because the selected workflow, weekly marketing performance reporting, required a clear point of initiation and traceability from the beginning of the process.

Upon submission, the system had to assign a unique workflow identifier. This requirement was critical because it allowed all subsequent activities, including execution logs, AI interactions, and final outputs, to be tied to a single auditable workflow instance.

3.3.1.2 Cross-Platform Workflow Execution
The system had to support execution of the same workflow across more than one automation runtime. In the prototype, this involved triggering workflow logic in Make and n8n. This function was central to the study because runtime independence could not be claimed unless the same governed process could be executed in more than one automation environment.

3.3.1.3 Execution Monitoring
The system had to monitor workflow execution events including workflow start, workflow completion, runtime status, and failure conditions. Monitoring information had to be captured independently of the automation platform in use. This requirement addressed the core problem of fragmented visibility across heterogeneous execution environments.

3.3.1.4 AI Interaction Logging
Because the workflow incorporated AI-generated summarisation, the system had to record prompts sent to the AI service and the responses returned. This requirement was essential for supporting transparency and accountability of AI-generated outputs. Without this logging mechanism, it would not be possible to reconstruct how the final report content was produced.

3.3.1.5 Performance Reporting
The system had to generate a final weekly performance report summarising the outcomes of the workflow. This report had to combine the operational data collected during workflow execution with the AI-generated analysis. This was necessary because the selected workflow was not only a technical process but also a marketing reporting task intended to support decision-making.

3.3.1.6 Error Detection and Logging
The system had to detect workflow anomalies and record errors in a central audit structure. This requirement supported the governance objective by ensuring that failures were not hidden داخل isolated platform logs. A governed workflow must not only execute but also surface its failures in a way that supports investigation.

3.3.2 Non-Functional Requirements
Non-functional requirements define the qualities the system must exhibit during operation. These requirements were especially important in this study because the research problem centred on governance, observability, and accountability rather than purely on workflow execution.

3.3.2.1 Transparency
The system had to provide clear visibility into workflow execution steps. A user or administrator should be able to determine what happened during workflow execution, which runtime performed the task, and what outputs were generated. Transparency was one of the key evaluation criteria identified in Chapter Two and is widely recognised as important in AI-enabled systems (Amershi et al., 2019; NIST, 2023).

3.3.2.2 Auditability
The system had to record sufficient information to reconstruct workflow execution history. Auditability required that execution records, AI interactions, timestamps, and status outcomes be preserved in a standardised and centralised form.

3.3.2.3 Scalability
The architecture had to support extension to additional automation platforms in future implementations. While the prototype focused on two runtimes, the architecture had to remain open enough to accommodate more platforms if the system were later expanded.

3.3.2.4 Platform Independence
The governance layer had to function independently of the automation runtime. This was not simply a technical preference. It was a core research requirement because the purpose of the study was to demonstrate that governance could be decoupled from workflow execution infrastructure.

3.3.2.5 Reliability
The system had to ensure that workflows executed consistently and that failures were detectable, recorded, and reviewable. Reliability in this context referred not only to successful workflow completion but also to dependable monitoring and traceability.

3.4 System Modelling
System modelling techniques were used to visually represent the system and support formal analysis of its structure and behaviour. In systems development, modelling helps translate abstract requirements into understandable representations of how a system will function (Dennis et al., 2021).

Three modelling techniques were used in this study:

Context Diagram

Level One Data Flow Diagram

Use Case Diagram

These techniques were selected because together they provide a balanced view of system boundaries, process flow, and actor interactions.

3.4.1 Context Diagram
The Context Diagram provided the highest-level abstraction of the proposed system. It represented the runtime-independent AgentOps platform as a single central process and showed its interaction with external entities. This type of model is useful because it clarifies system boundaries and identifies which external actors exchange information with the system (Dennis et al., 2021).

In this study, the external entities included:

Marketing Analyst

Automation Runtime

AI Processing Service

Reporting Dashboard

Central Data Repository

The context diagram established that the AgentOps platform was not itself the automation runtime. Instead, it functioned as the governance and monitoring layer positioned between users, runtimes, AI services, and reporting structures.

Figure 1: Context Diagram of the Runtime-Independent AgentOps Platform

The context diagram showed that the AgentOps platform functioned as the central governance layer linking workflow initiation, runtime execution, AI interaction, log storage, and reporting. This confirmed that governance and monitoring were externalised from the workflow execution environments.

3.4.2 Level One Data Flow Diagram
The Level One Data Flow Diagram decomposed the system into its major functional processes and data stores. While the context diagram showed the system as a single process, the Level One DFD showed how the platform internally handled workflow intake, execution, monitoring, logging, and reporting.

The system consisted of six major processes.

3.4.2.1 Process One: Campaign Intake and Validation
This process received workflow inputs from the marketing analyst and validated that the required information had been provided. It then created a structured workflow request and stored the initial record.

3.4.2.2 Process Two: Workflow Orchestration Control
This process determined which runtime environment would execute the workflow and issued the trigger instructions accordingly. It served as the coordination point between the governance layer and the automation runtimes.

3.4.2.3 Process Three: Execution Monitoring and Logging
This process collected runtime status updates and execution events. It standardised the information into a common monitoring structure and stored it centrally.

3.4.2.4 Process Four: AI Interaction Logging
This process recorded prompts submitted to the AI service and captured the resulting responses. This ensured that AI-generated content could be traced and audited later.

3.4.2.5 Process Five: Performance Aggregation
This process combined execution data and AI-generated outputs into a structured reporting dataset suitable for final analysis.

3.4.2.6 Process Six: Reporting and Feedback Generation
This process generated the final weekly report and dashboard-ready output. It transformed monitored workflow data into a usable marketing insight product.

The major data stores included:

Campaign Database

Audit Log Database

AI Interaction Log

Performance Metrics Repository

The Level One Data Flow Diagram illustrated the internal logic of the proposed platform. It showed how workflow requests were received, executed, monitored, logged, and transformed into reports. It also demonstrated how AI interactions and audit records were captured in separate but related data stores to improve traceability.

3.4.3 Use Case Diagram
The use case diagram identified the primary actors interacting with the system and the functions performed by the system in response to those actors. Use case modelling is useful in systems analysis because it presents system functionality from the perspective of users and external systems (Booch et al., 2007).

The primary actors in this study were:

Marketing Analyst

System Administrator

Automation Runtime

AI Service

The main use cases included:

Submit Campaign Brief

Approve Workflow

Trigger Cross-Platform Execution

Monitor Execution Status

Log AI Interaction

Review Audit Logs

Generate Weekly Report

View Performance Dashboard

Investigate Execution Error

These use cases reflected both operational functionality and governance requirements. For example, the ability to review audit logs and investigate errors showed that the system was designed not only to execute workflows but also to support accountability.
